The National Rural Health Association criticizes the proposal for explicitly declining to include flexibilities for rural providers, despite acknowledging they will have a harder time complying. Cleveland Clinic urges the Administration to "retract the current proposed rule and engage with stakeholders to devise requirements that align with safeguarding patient information while avoiding costly disruptions."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all trade associations and healthcare providers who addressed the topic.

exceptions.text: One advocacy group, ANCOR, "unequivocally agrees with the proposed rule's intent" to modernize cybersecurity but warns that its community provider members cannot comply without significant new funding and resources.

consensusPoints.text: HIPAA, in its current form, is outdated and requires modernization. A strong majority of stakeholders, from providers and payers to tech companies and small businesses, agree that rules designed in a pre-digital era are failing to meet the needs of modern healthcare. They point to misalignments with current technology, care models, and patient expectations. The National Small Business Association notes that key guidance hasn't been updated since 2006, despite data considerations becoming "nearly ubiquitous in business practices." Availity, L.L.C. argues that HIPAA, "designed in a pre-digital era, is no longer sufficient to govern a modern healthcare system." Dr. Margaret Chustecki states the Privacy Rule needs modernization to "better support current care coordination and research environments."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of organizational commenters.

exceptions.text: While agreeing on the need for modernization, patient-focused commenters often advocate for strengthening individual controls, whereas institutional commenters focus on streamlining rules to reduce burdens.

consensusPoints.text: Patient trust is essential for the future of digital health. A recurring theme across different stakeholder types is that without robust and clear privacy and security protections, patients will not engage with the digital tools and data sharing necessary for modern healthcare. Hims & Hers Health, Inc. believes "patient trust in digital health hinges on clear, fair privacy protections." The Federation of American Hospitals states that "trust in data privacy is essential for patient engagement in digital health." The Schizophrenia Policy Action Network notes that HIPAA was intended to "promot[e] trust to encourage seeking care."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders who discussed digital health adoption.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether the proposed HIPAA Security Rule updates should be completely withdrawn and replaced, or if the intent is sound but requires significant financial support for implementation.

positions.label: Reject and Replace
positions.stance: The proposed rule is fundamentally flawed, 'one-size-fits-all,' and must be withdrawn entirely in favor of a new, stakeholder-driven, flexible, risk-based approach. Representative voices include AAMC, UnitedHealth Group, and the Ohio Health Care Association.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority of institutional commenters (AHA, AAMC, AHIP, FAH, GNYHA, Cleveland Clinic, Ohio Health Care Assoc, NRHA, Point32Health).
keyArguments: The rule is operationally infeasible and prohibitively expensive ($9 billion+ first-year cost cited)
it diverts resources from proactive security to 'paperwork'
it disproportionately harms small and rural providers who could be put out of business.

positions.label: Good Intent, Bad Execution
positions.stance: The goal of strengthening cybersecurity is correct, but the proposed rule is unworkable for under-resourced providers without significant financial support and technical assistance. The representative voice is ANCOR.
positions.supportLevel: One I/DD provider advocacy group.
keyArguments: Community providers agree with the need to modernize systems but cannot comply with the short timeframe and high cost without new funding.

areasOfDebate.topic: HIPAA's Role in Care Coordination vs. Patient Control
areasOfDebate.description: This debate highlights the fundamental tension between using patient data to improve care coordination and value-based models, versus strengthening individual patient control and privacy rights to build trust and prevent misuse.

positions.label: Enable Coordination
positions.stance: HIPAA's ambiguity and misinterpretation actively hinder care coordination, value-based care, and caregiver involvement, requiring clarification to permit more seamless data sharing. Representative voices include Blue Cross Blue Shield Association, Schizophrenia Policy Action Network, and Dr. Margaret Chustecki.
positions.supportLevel: Many providers, health plans, and a key caregiver advocacy group.
keyArguments: Fear of violations prevents timely data sharing for population health (BCBSA)
the 'minimum necessary' rule is unworkable in integrated systems (GNYHA)
misinterpretation leads to 'disastrous consequences' for patients with severe mental illness by excluding caregivers (SPAN).

positions.label: Strengthen Patient Control
positions.stance: Current data sharing is already too broad and unrestricted, breaching patient privacy and eroding trust. Tighter controls and stronger patient rights are needed. Representative voices include Billie A. Glazier, R123 & R123News, Gail Romiti, and an anonymous individual.
positions.supportLevel: Patient advocates and some individuals.
keyArguments: 'Open records' policies lead to widespread, unrestricted sharing constituting a 'significant breach of patient privacy' (R123News)
internal access is 'far too broad,' allowing all staff to view sensitive records
individuals must have the right to opt-out and control who accesses their data.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the HIPAA Reproductive Health Rule, focusing on whether it is a necessary protection for patient privacy or a legally flawed overreach that creates conflicts for providers.

positions.label: Rescind Rule
positions.stance: The rule is an unnecessary, confusing, and legally flawed overreach that conflates abortion with healthcare and puts providers in an impossible legal position. Representative voices include National Association of Chain Drug Stores, Ethics and Public Policy Center, and Ethics and Religious Liberty Commission.
positions.supportLevel: Pharmacy association and religious/conscience-based advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Original HIPAA already protected this data
the rule creates substantial confusion, conflicts with state laws, and is based on a flawed premise
it forces providers to choose between violating HIPAA or defying subpoenas.

positions.label: Shield Data (Implicit)
positions.stance: The rule is necessary to protect patient privacy and prevent protected health information from being used in investigations or prosecutions related to reproductive healthcare. This position is not explicitly stated in the comments but is the clear intent of the rule, as described by its opponents.
positions.supportLevel: This position is not explicitly stated in the comments but is the clear intent of the rule, as described by its opponents.
keyArguments: The rule's purpose, as articulated by opponents, is to create a 'shield against laws regulating abortion' and prevent disclosure of PHI for investigations.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense administrative and financial burden of proposed and existing HIPAA rules, particularly the 'prohibitively costly' Security Rule and cumbersome documentation/notice requirements.
specificPoints: They consistently frame regulatory burdens as resources diverted directly from patient care, innovation, and workforce support. They are most vocal about the operational infeasibility of 'one-size-fits-all' mandates in complex, varied healthcare environments (e.g., rural vs. urban).

Withdraw the proposed Security Rule; replace prescriptive rules with flexible, risk-based standards; eliminate requirements with low patient benefit (e.g., NPP acknowledgment); align 42 CFR Part 2 with HIPAA.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ambiguity in HIPAA hindering data sharing for value-based care and population health management; the high cost and prescriptive nature of the proposed Security Rule.
specificPoints: They focus on how data-sharing barriers prevent them from improving system efficiency and lowering costs for members. BCBSA provides a detailed case that clarifying the 'health care operations' definition is critical for chronic condition management and preventing duplicate services.
Clarify HIPAA to explicitly permit data sharing for care coordination; withdraw the proposed Security Rule; allow electronic breach notifications.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of individual control over health data; overly broad and unrestricted access to records by internal staff; specific privacy violations; and, conversely, caregivers being denied crucial information for loved ones with severe mental illness.
specificPoints: Their input is grounded in personal experience and a rights-based framework. They express deep mistrust and articulate the human cost of privacy failures and information blocking.

Grant patients the legal right to document in their own records; implement strict internal access controls; create an explicit HIPAA exception for caregivers of incapacitated SMI patients; ensure the right to anonymous, self-paid testing.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Outdated HIPAA rules stifling innovation; lack of clear guidance for modern technologies (cloud, text messaging, AI); the misclassification of clearinghouses as Business Associates instead of Covered Entities, which limits their ability to improve the system.
specificPoints: They view the healthcare system through a lens of data flow and technological potential, arguing that regulatory inertia creates massive waste and blocks transformative improvements.
Formally recognize clearinghouses as Covered Entities with full TPO rights; issue clear, modern guidance on digital tools; repeal the obsolete de-identification standard.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The disproportionate and potentially fatal impact of one-size-fits-all, high-cost regulations on small entities that lack dedicated IT and compliance departments.
specificPoints: They are the 'canary in the coal mine,' warning that rules designed for large urban hospitals will shutter essential rural and small practices.
Withdraw the proposed Security Rule; create a 'cybersecurity safe harbor' for small businesses with compliant programs.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The 'Covered Entity vs. Business Associate' Paradox: Availity points out that while healthcare clearinghouses are legally defined as Covered Entities, they are functionally treated as Business Associates. This subtle distinction has massive consequences, preventing them from using their unique network-wide view to aggregate data, innovate on payment models, or proactively detect fraud and waste, adding immense friction and cost to the system.

Clinical Condition as a Driver of Policy Failure: The Schizophrenia Policy Action Network provides a powerful insight by linking a specific clinical symptom—anosognosia (a lack of insight into one's illness)—directly to the failure of HIPAA implementation. This transforms the debate from a legalistic argument to a life-or-death patient safety issue, showing how a well-intentioned privacy rule can have 'disastrous consequences' when it fails to account for the realities of severe mental illness.

The Obsolescence of De-identification Standards: The End Burnout Group argues that the HIPAA de-identification standard is not just outdated but fundamentally broken. Developed in the 1990s, it fails to account for modern data aggregation capabilities (e.g., cell phone GPS data) that make re-identification trivial. This insight challenges the very foundation of how de-identified data is used for research and commerce.

Records as a 'Legal Shield': Billie Glazier of R123News offers a deeply cynical but critical perspective: that shared medical records are being 'us[ed] as legal shields rather than tools of healing.' This suggests a cultural rot where data sharing, intended to improve care, is instead being co-opted for institutional self-protection, undermining patient trust and the integrity of second opinions.

'Re-victimizing' Cyberattack Victims: Point32Health makes the compelling point that current responses to cyberattacks, which involve extensive audits and compliance actions, effectively 're-victimiz[e]' the healthcare entities that were targeted, diverting resources from recovery to administrative defense at the worst possible time.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type patterns: A stark divide exists between large, well-resourced health systems and small/rural providers. The latter group, represented by the NRHA and Ohio Health Care Association, warns that the 'one-size-fits-all' approach of the proposed Security Rule is an existential threat, while larger systems like Ascension and Cleveland Clinic focus more on the operational burdens and diversion of resources.

Unintended consequences: Multiple commenters highlight how HIPAA-related policies have backfired. The OCR bulletin on online tracking technologies led to a 'flood of litigation' against hospitals (GNYHA). The HIPAA Reproductive Health Rule created 'substantial confusion' and 'delays in medication access' (NACDS). Overly cautious HIPAA interpretations are directly harming patients with severe mental illness (SPAN).

Technology-Regulation Mismatch: A pervasive theme is that HIPAA is a 20th-century law struggling to govern 21st-century technology. Commenters from the tech sector (Connected Health Initiative, Availity) and even clinical groups (End Burnout Group) point out that the rules are ill-equipped to handle cloud computing, text messaging, AI, and modern data analytics, leading to a state of paralysis and fear that stifles innovation.

The 'Weaponization' of HIPAA: Two distinct groups describe how the rule is being used for purposes other than its original intent. Availity claims HIPAA is used 'as a weapon to block beneficial uses of data.' Conversely, opponents of the Reproductive Health Rule (EPPC) argue it turns HIPAA into a 'shield against laws regulating abortion.' This pattern shows the rule becoming a tool in broader institutional and political conflicts.

keyQuotations.quote: [The proposed HIPAA Security Rule would] necessitate ‘near-continuous document review and updating,’ causing cybersecurity professionals to spend time on paperwork instead of ‘critical, proactive efforts’ to protect patient information from cyber threats.
For small providers who do not have information technology departments familiar with the concepts in the proposed rule, the cost of compliance could put them out of business.

What was meant to serve the patient is increasingly being used to obstruct the very innovations that would improve patient care, efficiency, and access. HIPAA is now too often invoked not as a shield for the patient, but as a weapon to block beneficial uses of data.
Tragically, healthcare providers rarely use allowable HIPAA exceptions for people with schizophrenia, leading to disastrous consequences.
It is long past time to end this regulation that allows your private health data to be shared with others without your knowledge, let alone without your approval.

[There is a] growing culture within the medical community of using shared medical records as legal shields rather than tools of healing... Rather than correcting prior missteps, many providers appear to prioritize institutional protection—defending one another's actions instead of advocating for what is medically right.
We believe that the six-year HIPAA record retention requirement... is unnecessarily long and is burdensome... a period of time during which the retained information is vulnerable to data breaches.
The new 'presumption' provision... puts healthcare providers 'in an impossible situation'—risking contempt for refusing a subpoena or risking HIPAA violation.
Health care preserves human life while abortion ends it. To conflate the two is unjust...

A national standard for electronic attachments will reduce administrative friction, lower costs, accelerate payment cycles, and bring dental claim workflows in line with medical claims. This is a low-cost, high-impact change...
It's ridiculous that I can't get any testing anonymously even when I am self paying....talk about privacy rights???
There must be zero contact or transfer of information from any government agency to any private entity about American's health information.

5.5.themeDescription: Electronic Health Record (EHR) and Health IT Certification

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus that the ONC Health IT Certification Program and associated regulations like the Promoting Interoperability Program have become excessively burdensome, stifle innovation, and prioritize check-box compliance over clinical usability, a major driver of provider burnout. The key tension is between stakeholders calling for the complete elimination of programs and those advocating for a more surgical approach of rescinding specific low-value criteria. The dominant recommendations are to immediately rescind the Insights Condition and Real World Testing requirements, shift program requirements from rigid measures to simple attestations, and refocus the entire certification model on real-world interoperability, user-centered design, and patient outcomes.

consensusPoints.text: The ONC Certification Program is overly burdensome and has outlived its original purpose. A vast majority of commenters, from individual physicians to large hospital associations and health IT vendors, agree that the program's requirements are excessive, outdated, and often deliver little value. Netsmart states that ONC regulatory requirements add burden to providers and Health Information Technology (Health IT) developers without clear benefits to either entity or to patients. Epic argues that certain criteria have outlived its utility and cause clinicians to spend significant time testing unchanged software. The Federation of American Hospitals (FAH) notes the Promoting Interoperability Program adds administrative burden without delivering proportional value and urges HHS to phase out or significantly simplify it.

exceptions.text: While highly critical, several health IT vendors (Netsmart, Oracle) noted that they value the certification testing tools provided by ONC and urged their continued support and modernization.

consensusPoints.text: The Certification Program prioritizes compliance over clinical usability, contributing to provider burnout. There is broad agreement that EHRs have been designed to meet federal certification requirements rather than the needs of end-users, resulting in poor usability that is a primary source of provider dissatisfaction. Dr. Chustecki states that EHR evolution has 'prioritized billing/reporting/compliance over clinical usability,' with non-clinical tasks consuming 30-50% of clinicians’ EHR time and contributing to early retirement. The Physicians Advocacy Institute (PAI) observes that EHR system enhancements 'focus on meeting federal certification requirements instead of user innovation'. The Connected Health Initiative argues that providers are bound to use poorly functioning Certified Electronic Health Record Technology (CEHRT) products—built primarily to measure and report on CMS requirements—and are disincentivized from adopting truly useful technology.

The 'Insights Condition' and 'Real World Testing' are prime examples of low-value, high-burden requirements that should be rescinded. A strong majority of commenters, particularly health IT developers and their associations, specifically targeted these two Conditions of Certification for elimination. Epic describes the Insights Condition as highly burdensome, lack[ing] clear benefits to developers and users, and notes that Real World Testing requires an estimated 450 hours annually for reporting, not including development burden. Netsmart recommends the complete elimination of the Insights condition of certification due to its cumbersome and overly burdensome reporting structure. The HIMSS EHRA, representing nearly 30 EHR companies, states these conditions impose burdensome requirements that offer limited value to providers or patients.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether to completely eliminate burdensome programs like Promoting Interoperability or to surgically refine them by rescinding specific low-value criteria while retaining the overall certification framework.

positions.label: Eliminate & Replace
positions.stance: Argues that programs like Promoting Interoperability (PI) are fundamentally broken and should be eliminated entirely.
positions.supportLevel: Most hospital associations and some provider groups.
keyArguments: The program has devolved from an incentive into a 'penalty program' (GNYHA)
it has 'outlived its usefulness' (AHA)
its complexity adds burden without proportional value (FAH).

positions.label: Surgically Refine
positions.stance: Argues for a more targeted approach, rescinding specific low-value criteria while retaining the overall certification framework.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly all health IT vendors and their associations.
keyArguments: A targeted approach can remove the worst burdens (Insights, RWT) while preserving valuable components like standardized interoperability and testing tools
allows for incremental improvement without disrupting the entire ecosystem.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense development and reporting burden from specific, low-value certification criteria (Insights, Real World Testing, (g)(3) summative testing); outdated testing procedures; and misaligned compliance timelines that give providers no time to adapt.
specificPoints: They provide a detailed, 'inside' view of the technical and resource costs of compliance, such as Epic's estimate of 450 hours for a single reporting requirement.
They also have a nuanced appreciation for the value of ONC's testing tools, even while criticizing the regulations they support.
Rescind specific burdensome criteria, modernize ONC's testing tools, and create a 'flex year' between vendor and provider compliance deadlines (Oracle).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The Promoting Interoperability (PI) Program has become a costly penalty program; eCQM reporting is inaccurate and time-consuming; and EHR vendors restrict access to their own data, hindering analytics and population health initiatives.
specificPoints: They highlight the large-scale operational impact of these regulations, citing specific staff hours wasted on data validation (Ascension spent 61 hours/month per hospital) and the disadvantages faced by small and rural facilities (FAH).
Eliminate or drastically simplify the PI program, make eCQM reporting voluntary, and mandate that vendors provide seamless data access.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Poor EHR usability is a primary driver of clinician burnout and dissatisfaction; the high cost of acquiring and maintaining certified EHRs threatens the viability of small and independent practices; and certification criteria are not relevant to specialty workflows.
specificPoints: They connect regulatory policy directly to the clinical experience and the professional well-being of physicians.
The Physicians Advocacy Institute (PAI) uniquely links the cost of CEHRT mandates to the acceleration of healthcare consolidation.
Reform certification to prioritize user-centered design, replace numerator/denominator reporting with simple 'yes/no' attestations, and prohibit vendors from charging for mandatory updates.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The 'one-size-fits-all' certification model is inapplicable and burdensome for their settings (e.g., behavioral health, oral surgery, retail clinics, labs). A lack of technical standards for their specific needs (e.g., electronic signatures for lab orders) creates significant administrative friction.
specificPoints: They reveal major gaps in the regulatory framework, where entire segments of healthcare are penalized for using systems not designed for their work.
Netsmart provides a powerful example of PTs and OTs being forced to certify for e-prescribing, a function they don't use.

Create certification pathways for specialty settings, simplify requirements for episodic care (CCA), and convene stakeholders to establish new technical standards for things like lab order e-signatures (ACLA, Quest).

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Physicians Advocacy Institute (PAI) makes a compelling case that the high cost of acquiring and maintaining federally mandated CEHRT is a direct factor 'accelerat[ing] physician employment and healthcare consolidation,' as independent practices cannot keep up.

Point32Health provides a crucial market analysis, arguing that EHR vendor consolidation has created 'anti-competitive ways for vendors to maximize revenues, including financial barriers to data sharing.' This frames data blocking not just as an interoperability problem, but as a market-power problem.

Oracle Health’s proposal for a 'flex year' is a simple, elegant solution to the chronic problem where providers are held accountable for using new certified technology almost immediately after vendors are required to make it available, leaving no time for implementation, workflow redesign, or training.

ACLA and Quest Diagnostics don't just complain about a problem (lack of e-signatures for lab orders); they identify the specific technical mismatch (HL7v2 vs. CDA) that causes it, offering a clear target for a solution.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Views diverge significantly by practice size and type. Large hospitals and health systems (AHA, FAH, Ascension) focus their critique on the operational burden of large-scale programs like Promoting Interoperability and eCQM reporting. In contrast, smaller and specialty practices (PAI, AAOMS, ACG) are more concerned with the fundamental cost of entry for CEHRT and the lack of suitable products for their specific workflows.

The very programs designed to promote EHR adoption and interoperability (Meaningful Use/PI) are now cited as primary drivers of burnout, innovation-stifling 'check-box' development (PAI), and market consolidation (PAI). The regulation has created perverse incentives that undermine its original goals.

Earlier regulatory efforts focused on mandating what functionalities an EHR must have. The overwhelming sentiment now is to shift focus to how the technology performs in the real world—its usability, its actual interoperability, and its impact on outcomes—and to provide far more flexibility in achieving those ends (e.g., attestation).

keyQuotations.quote: [EHR evolution has] prioritized billing/reporting/compliance over clinical usability... [These mandates] reduce face-to-face interaction, fragment care delivery, and are a top contributor to professional dissatisfaction and early retirement in primary care.
The Promoting Interoperability (PI) Program has evolved into a 'penalty program' from its original design to incentivize the adoption of certified electronic health record (EHR) technology (CEHRT).

Since the inception of the ONC certification program, health IT developers who specialize in developing software for providers in the human services, behavioral health, post-acute and other specialty practice settings have been constrained and required to certify to criteria that is wholly inapplicable to these health care providers, facilities and patients served.

Certification mandates for EHRs inadvertently create compliance and operating costs for small practices because EHR vendors charge for each update and regular maintenance, even if these updates are not relevant.

Although EHRs efficiently manage individual patient records, they remain limited in supporting broader objectives such as population health management, advanced analytics, and quality improvement initiatives.
At one Ascension hospital, staff spent an average of 61 hours per month validating the minimal number of cases required for eCQMs.
[We urge] complete elimination of the Insights condition of certification due to its cumbersome and overly burdensome reporting structure.

CMS should create a distinction in timing requirements between when certified health IT capabilities must be available for developers to deliver and when providers must begin using them... CMS should allow a year of flexibility (a 'flex year').

[P]rogram participants are now bound to use poorly functioning Certified Electronic Health Record Technology (CEHRT) products—built primarily to measure and report on CMS requirements—and are disincentivized from adopting truly useful technology.
Without stronger industry accountability, current reporting risks undermining its intent and further straining healthcare providers.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on AI in healthcare is overwhelmingly one of dualism: immense optimism for its potential to revolutionize care, mixed with significant fear of its misuse. The core tension is between innovators, who see regulatory uncertainty and overreach as the primary barriers chilling progress, and providers and advocates, who demand strong guardrails to prevent patient harm from biased algorithms and automated denials. The most contentious frontier is the FDA's guidance on Clinical Decision Support (CDS) software, which numerous stakeholders argue unlawfully expands jurisdiction, creating a chilling effect on the integration of valuable tools into EHRs.

consensusPoints.text: AI holds transformative potential. Nearly all commenters agree that AI offers significant opportunities to improve healthcare. This includes streamlining administrative tasks, enhancing clinical trials, improving diagnostics, personalizing care, and increasing efficiency. Support for this view spans all stakeholder types, from tech developers to patient advocates. For example, Premier Inc. sees "particular promise for AI in streamlining processes and expanding patient access in clinical trials". Patients For Patient Safety US envisions AI as a "'Central Nervous System' for safety in healthcare". Even critics of current AI use, like the Physicians Advocacy Institute, believe AI "should be leveraged to improve timely access to evidence-based care".

Regulatory uncertainty is a major barrier to innovation. A strong majority of commenters, particularly from industry, technology, and research sectors, state that ambiguity in current regulations is a primary obstacle to developing and deploying beneficial AI tools. This uncertainty is described as having a "chilling effect" on investment and progress. For example, Availity states, "It is not a technical limitation stifling innovation but the regulatory uncertainty serving as the barrier". Premier Inc. argues that "Regulatory uncertainty...is chilling innovation" in clinical trials. Wolters Kluwer notes that new product innovations "likely already have been, abandoned due to regulatory uncertainty".

exceptions.text: While most agree uncertainty is a problem, they diverge on the solution, with some calling for deregulation and safe harbors, and others demanding clearer, stronger rules.

consensusPoints.text: Guardrails are necessary to prevent misuse and harm. Most stakeholders, including providers, patient groups, and even some industry commenters, acknowledge the risks of AI and agree on the need for protective guardrails. Concerns focus on automated denials of care, algorithmic bias, and patient safety. For example, The American Medical Association (AMA) recommends creating "regulatory guardrails around AI use in Prior Authorization decisions". Dr. Margaret Chustecki warns that a lack of oversight leads to "inconsistent performance and potential bias". Cleveland Clinic calls for guardrails "to prevent inappropriate AI use in coverage decisions".

areasOfDebate.topic: FDA Regulation of Clinical Decision Support (CDS) Software
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether the FDA's 2022 guidance unlawfully expands its jurisdiction over low-risk CDS, thereby stifling innovation, or provides necessary oversight for patient safety.

positions.label: Deregulate / Rescind
positions.stance: FDA has unlawfully expanded its jurisdiction over low-risk CDS, stifling innovation.
positions.supportLevel: All major health IT developers and their trade associations (Epic, Wolters Kluwer, HIMSS Electronic Health Record Association).
keyArguments: The 2022 FDA guidance introduces non-statutory concepts like 'time-criticality' and 'automation bias';
it misinterprets the 21st Century Cures Act's intent to exempt low-risk tools;
classifying common EHR features as medical devices imposes prohibitive costs and delays, causing developers to abandon projects.

positions.label: Strengthen Oversight
positions.stance: Clear oversight is essential to ensure AI tools are transparent, equitable, and safe.
positions.supportLevel: Some healthcare providers and academics.
keyArguments: Unregulated AI tools pose risks of inconsistent performance and bias, undermining ethical governance;

transparency and bias-mitigation strategies must be required to protect patients. (Note: This position doesn't explicitly defend the current FDA guidance but argues against a purely deregulatory approach).

areasOfDebate.topic: AI in Claims and Prior Authorization Review
areasOfDebate.description: Conflicting views on the use of AI in claims and prior authorization, pitting patient and provider advocates against health plans and clearinghouses.

positions.label: Restrict / Prohibit
positions.stance: Ban AI-driven treatment denials without human medical review.
positions.supportLevel: Physician and patient advocacy groups, some conservative advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Opaque algorithms are being used by insurers to wrongfully deny necessary care;
this practice harms patients and must be stopped;
audits of these algorithms are needed.

positions.label: Leverage for Integrity
positions.stance: Use AI to detect fraud, overpayments, and improve administrative efficiency.
positions.supportLevel: Health plans, clearinghouses, and some advocacy groups (in the context of government program integrity).
keyArguments: AI can identify hard-to-find patterns of fraud and waste, making integrity programs more efficient and saving taxpayer dollars;
it can streamline administrative transactions, reducing costs for the entire system.

areasOfDebate.topic: Data Use for AI Model Development
areasOfDebate.description: The tension between establishing clear safe harbors to use protected health information for innovation versus mandating disclosure and bias mitigation strategies.

positions.label: Clarify & Protect
positions.stance: Create clear guidance and safe harbors for using PHI to train AI, particularly de-identified data.
positions.supportLevel: Technology companies, data clearinghouses, and life science organizations.
keyArguments: HIPAA’s ambiguity has a chilling effect, leading organizations to prohibit AI use contractually even for permitted functions;
clear rules are needed to unlock innovation in treatment, payment, and operations (TPO).

positions.label: Mandate & Disclose
positions.stance: Require disclosure of data sources and mandate bias-mitigation strategies.
positions.supportLevel: Some providers and health insurers (in the context of certification).
keyArguments: To ensure safety and equity, programs using HHS funds must disclose their AI use and submit bias mitigation plans;
transparency into data sources and model logic is necessary for accountability.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Developers & Tech Trade Associations
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory overreach (especially FDA's CDS guidance), legal ambiguity (HIPAA, Anti-Kickback), and duplicative compliance burdens that stifle innovation.
specificPoints: They argue that regulation, not technology, is the primary bottleneck. They provide detailed, statute-level critiques of specific guidance documents.
Rescind the 2022 FDA CDS guidance; issue clear guidance creating safe harbors for data use; adopt a risk-based, technology-neutral approach.
Epic's detailed takedown of the FDA's CDS guidance, arguing it "defies common sense" and threatens over 150,000 existing CDS tools used by its clients.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Misuse of AI by payers for automated claim denials; algorithmic bias in clinical tools; lack of transparency and accountability.
specificPoints: They are on the front lines, experiencing the negative impacts of opaque payer algorithms and worrying about the reliability of tools they are asked to use. They also highlight the problem of critical data being "inaccessible to advanced analytics and AI applications" within their own EHRs, as noted by Ascension.
Ban AI-driven denials without human review; mandate algorithmic transparency and bias mitigation strategies; establish guardrails for payer use of AI.
Cleveland Clinic's concern that payers use algorithms to "re-audit previous claims until an issue supports a denial".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Duplicative and confusing regulations (e.g., nondiscrimination rules for AI, DSI transparency); premature mandates that are not risk-based.
specificPoints: While supporting AI for efficiency, they are focused on fending off what they see as new, unnecessary regulatory layers that create compliance burdens without adding value.
Rescind specific rules (42 CFR §92.210); rely on existing laws and collaborative, standards-based approaches rather than prescriptive mandates.
AHIP's call to rescind the 'patient care decision support tools' provision in the Nondiscrimination Final Rule, arguing it "duplicates other consumer protection provisions".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty hindering the use of AI in clinical trials (synthetic control arms, patient recruitment) and drug manufacturing.
specificPoints: This group is focused on the R&D pipeline. They uniquely highlight how statutes like the Anti-Kickback law create uncertainty for using AI to identify trial participants, which could accelerate drug development.

Issue clear FDA guidance on using RWD/synthetic data for control arms; clarify the applicability of the Anti-Kickback statute to AI in drug development; provide safe harbor exceptions for technology use in R&D.
Premier Inc. urging the FDA to "clarify how the anti-kickback statute applies to AI technology in drug development" to remove regulatory uncertainty.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Regulation, Not Technology, is the Bottleneck: Availity makes the sharp observation that "It is not a technical limitation stifling innovation but the regulatory uncertainty serving as the barrier." They add that fear of contractual disputes, not just government enforcement, drives this risk aversion.

The Anti-Kickback Risk in Clinical Trials: Premier Inc. highlights a sophisticated and critical barrier: uncertainty over whether using AI to identify and recruit trial participants could be construed as a prohibited "kickback," hindering efforts to accelerate drug development.

Time-Based Billing as a Structural Barrier: The Alliance for Connected Care provides a fundamental insight: "Coding based on the time a clinician spends rather than the outcome they achieve hinders the use of artificial intelligence." This points to a core economic misalignment that discourages automation.

A Detailed Deconstruction of FDA's Guidance: Epic offers an exceptionally detailed legal and practical critique of the FDA's 2022 CDS guidance, breaking down how the agency's interpretation of each of the four statutory criteria allegedly subverts the intent of Congress.

Extreme Public Fear: The comment from Targeted Justice, alleging the use of "IA interface Havana syndrom" and "V2K IA" for persecution, represents a profound outlier. While not a mainstream policy concern, it illustrates the extreme end of the spectrum of public anxiety regarding the malicious and non-consensual use of AI technology.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A Stark Innovator vs. Protector Divide: A clear pattern emerges with two primary camps. Innovators (tech developers, life sciences) see the main threat as over-regulation and ambiguity, which stifles progress. Protectors (providers, patient advocates) see the main threat as under-regulation and misuse, which causes patient harm.

The Duality of AI: Multiple commenters view the same AI capability through two lenses. For example, AI for claims analysis is seen as a positive tool for government fraud detection but a negative weapon for insurer claim denials. This highlights that the perceived value and risk of AI is highly dependent on the user's role and incentives.

Frustration with Siloed Data: A pattern of frustration exists among providers like Ascension, who note that even within their own systems, critical operational data in EHRs remains "inaccessible to advanced analytics and AI applications," requiring costly workarounds and limiting quality improvement efforts.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] interpretation that CDS software fails this criterion by providing a single recommendation defies common sense. In many cases, there is just one clinically appropriate recommendation for healthcare providers to adopt or disregard.

New product innovations and features, possibly including AI-enablement, could be (and likely already have been) abandoned, thus depriving providers and patients of enhanced functionality that could improve health outcomes and lower costs.

It is not a technical limitation stifling innovation but the regulatory uncertainty serving as the barrier. Organizations fear that even permitted uses under HIPAA may result in unpredictable enforcement or contractual disputes.
The resulting ambiguity has a chilling effect on developers’ willingness to invest in analytics and CDS tools needed to support public health and value-based care programs.
Concerns exist about AI use to downgrade service levels, especially for emergency department and inpatient care. Algorithms are used to re-audit previous claims until an issue supports a denial.
AI as a 'Central Nervous System' for safety in health care, enabling real-time detection of high-risk patterns and signals without manual input.
Coding based on the time a clinician spends rather than the outcome they achieve hinders the use of artificial intelligence.
It is imperative that AI use starts with pilot programs that are controlled studies to assess efficiency and costs to the healthcare system.
Transactional EHRs house thousands of distinct data elements, resulting in critical operational data being inaccessible to advanced analytics and AI applications.

Premier strongly recommends the FDA provide a clearly defined exception for the use of data science and technology to safely develop new drugs more cost-effectively and efficiently, removing regulatory uncertainty and promoting American leadership.
I am Missing my biologiske twins Anora and Asbjørn every day in Denmark and they use Havana syndrom V2K IA with out my consent.
CMS is simultaneously refusing to allow HHAs to improve their efficiency while creating newfound regulatory burden.

6.1.themeDescription: Scheduling and Classification of Controlled Substances. This theme captures the extensive debate over the scheduling of controlled substances, particularly ADHD medications. || It includes arguments that the Schedule II classification for stimulants is outdated, not based on current science (e.g., DSM-5), and inappropriately groups therapeutic medications with dangerous narcotics. It also covers critiques of the Eight-Factor Analysis (8FA) and the erosion of the separation of powers between HHS (scientific) and DEA (enforcement)

sections.executiveSummary: Public input on this theme reveals a near-unanimous and urgent demand to reclassify ADHD stimulant medications from Schedule II to a less restrictive schedule. An overwhelming majority of commenters, primarily patients with lived experience, argue the current classification is outdated, scientifically baseless, and inappropriately groups therapeutic drugs with dangerous narcotics, leading to immense stigma and critical barriers to care. The dominant recommendation is to immediately re-evaluate and reclassify these medications to Schedule III or IV, with a secondary, more expert-driven recommendation to reform the underlying regulatory process by restoring the separation of powers between HHS and DEA.

consensusPoints.text: The Schedule II classification for ADHD medications is outdated and scientifically unjustified. Commenters repeatedly argue the classification is based on "fear-driven policy from the 1970s", has not kept up with "current science or patient realities", and ignores modern medical understanding, such as the DSM-5 removing "amphetamine addiction as a diagnosis years ago".

exceptions.text: While this view is nearly universal among individuals, no comments from regulatory bodies or law enforcement were provided to offer a counter-perspective.

consensusPoints.text: Grouping ADHD medications with dangerous narcotics like fentanyl and opioids is illogical and harmful. "It boggles my mind that ADHD medications like Adderall and Ritalin are still locked in the same category as fentanyl, methadone, Vicodin, Percocet, and OxyContin". "Why is Adderall treated like fentanyl? That stuff kills people. I’m just trying to keep my head clear and my daughter fed". "The idea that they’re grouped in the same category as fentanyl or oxy is not just outdated - it’s dangerous".

The Schedule II classification is the root cause of access barriers and patient stigma. Commenters state the classification prevents early refills or prescription transfers, makes "pharmacists nervous to fill" and "doctors hesitant to prescribe", and ultimately makes patients "feel like criminals for needing basic treatment".

Patients with ADHD feel they are being treated like criminals or drug-seekers. "The law treats me like a risk, not a patient". "Stop treating people with ADHD like we’re one mistake away from a criminal record just for needing medication to get through the day". "But for ADHD? We get treated like we’re gaming the system".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A significant majority of individuals with ADHD

areasOfDebate.description: Given the overwhelming consensus, there is no substantive debate on the core issue of whether to reclassify ADHD medications; support is virtually unanimous. The debates that do exist are more nuanced, focusing on the appropriate new schedule and the underlying cause of the regulatory failure.

positions.label: Move to Schedule III
positions.stance: A specific recommendation for reclassification to Schedule III.
positions.supportLevel: A notable portion of commenters, including a veteran and other individuals.
keyArguments: This would provide necessary relief from the most severe restrictions of Schedule II while still acknowledging the need for control.
It aligns with the classification of other controlled substances like testosterone.

positions.label: Move to Schedule IV
positions.stance: A recommendation for a more significant reclassification, often by comparing ADHD stimulants to benzodiazepines.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but vocal group of individuals.
keyArguments: Argues that drugs like Xanax (Schedule IV) are perceived as more addictive yet are less restricted, making Schedule IV a more logical placement for stimulants.

positions.label: DEA's Fault
positions.stance: The DEA's enforcement mindset and outdated rules are the primary problem.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of individual commenters.
keyArguments: The DEA is "clinging to rules written in the 1970s".
The agency is fighting a "war on ADHD disguised as drug control".
Its regulations are based on "fear, not facts".

positions.label: HHS's Failure
positions.stance: The core issue is HHS's "abdication" of its scientific authority, allowing the DEA to dominate scheduling.
positions.supportLevel: A small group of highly informed policy experts.
keyArguments: Cites the "collapse of the intended separation of powers" between HHS (scientific) and DEA (enforcement).
HHS has underutilized its "categorical drug control veto" and fails to provide independent scientific review, effectively "rubber-stamping" DEA decisions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Feeling stigmatized and criminalized; practical barriers to accessing medication (e.g., inability to transfer scripts, get early refills); the illogical grouping of their therapeutic medication with dangerous narcotics; the emotional toll of navigating a hostile system.
specificPoints: This group provides visceral, firsthand accounts of how scheduling directly impacts their daily lives, job performance, family stability, and mental health. They often express a sense of betrayal and frustration that a system meant to ensure health is instead causing harm and chaos.
Overwhelmingly, they call for reclassifying ADHD medications to Schedule III or IV and for regulators to "Stop treating people with ADHD like criminals".

The welder who states, "The Schedule II status is killing people’s stability". The veteran who remembers when the schedules were created and says they "were based on fear, not facts". The mother who feels "treated like a criminal at the pharmacy".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The systemic and procedural flaws in the scheduling process; the 'one-way ratcheting effect' that favors upscheduling; the erosion of HHS's statutory scientific authority; the use of asymmetric standards for evidence by the DEA.
specificPoints: These commenters provide a structural critique of the regulatory framework itself, citing legal precedent (Grinspoon v. DEA), statutory history, and data on scheduling actions over decades. They diagnose the problem not just as a bad outcome, but a broken process.
Restore the intended separation of powers between HHS and DEA; force a re-evaluation of substances based on rigorous, symmetric scientific evidence.
The detailed analysis of the "collapse of the intended separation of powers" and the data-backed observation of the "one-way ratcheting effect" of scheduling actions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The chilling effect of Schedule II on prescribers; the irrationality of the scheduling system when compared to clinical reality; being forced to navigate a "fear-based" system that harms patients.
specificPoints: They see the system from both a professional and, in some cases, a personal patient perspective. They witness firsthand how "PCPs feel like they will be criminalized for helping patients".
Revise the drug control framework to align with modern medical consensus; reclassify ADHD medications.
The Registered Nurse with 25+ years of experience who calls the current policy "fear-based policy from the 1970s, not science".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Diversion as a Symptom, Not a Cause: A recurring and powerful re-framing of the diversion issue. Multiple commenters suggest that if diversion is happening, "maybe that says more about how broken the system is" because "restrictive laws force people into desperation". This turns the primary justification for strict controls on its head.

The 'One-Way Ratchet' of Scheduling: The expert analysis provides a compelling data-backed framework for understanding regulatory inertia. The idea that agencies use asymmetric evidence standards—accepting low-quality evidence to up-schedule but requiring high-quality evidence to down-schedule—explains why classifications from the 1970s persist.

A War on Patients, Not Drugs: Several commenters articulated a feeling that the regulatory posture is no longer about public safety but has become a punitive "war on ADHD patients" or a "war on ADHD disguised as drug control", shifting the focus from a policy problem to a perceived persecution of a vulnerable group.

Compelling Historical Context: The veteran who is "old enough to remember when these schedules were made" and states they "were based on fear, not facts" provides a unique and credible voice grounded in decades of observation.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Experience Correlation: A clear pattern emerges where the focus of the critique depends on the commenter's role. Patients focus on the emotional toll and practical barriers (stigma, pharmacy denials). Policy experts focus on the procedural breakdown and statutory interpretation (HHS/DEA power struggle). Healthcare professionals focus on the chilling effect on medical practice. All three perspectives converge on the same conclusion: the current scheduling is wrong and must be changed.

Unintended Consequences: The most frequently cited unintended consequence is that the extreme difficulty of legal access may be causing the very diversion it's meant to prevent, as stable patients are forced into "desperate situations" when their legal supply is cut off. A second is the chilling effect on prescribers, leading to a "crisis of under-treatment".

keyQuotations.quote: [I]t feels like there’s a war on ADHD, not drug misuse.
The law treats me like a risk, not a patient.

It boggles my mind that ADHD medications like Adderall and Ritalin are still locked in the same category as fentanyl, methadone, Vicodin, Percocet, and OxyContin - drugs that are known to cause fatal overdoses and widespread addiction.

The Schedule II label doesn’t just affect policy - it affects how people see us. It makes pharmacists suspicious, doctors hesitant, and patients like me feel like criminals for needing basic treatment.

How can a medication that has helped so many people, for over 80 years, still be grouped with fentanyl and Vicodin? Meanwhile, benzos like Xanax are Schedule IV, and testosterone is Schedule III. That alone proves the scheduling isn’t based on rational, up-to-date medical standards.

The collapse of the intended separation of powers between the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) and the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) in controlled substance scheduling has created a public health crisis.
If these meds were truly so addictive, where are all the Adderall junkies? I’ve never met one. What I see are working folks... just trying to stay on top of our jobs, families, and responsibilities.

The system acts like it’s protecting people from abuse, but in reality, it’s punishing people who use these medications responsibly. All because of a label written for a tiny percentage of potential abusers. In the process, 100% of real patients suffer. That’s not protecting people - it’s failing them.
If diversion is happening, maybe that’s a sign the system’s too rigid, not too loose.
I’m old enough to remember when these schedules were made. They were based on fear, not facts.

What I don’t get is why these medications are treated like dangerous narcotics. They’ve been used since the 1940s... If they were truly addictive, we’d have half the country hooked, but that’s not what’s happening.

Agencies require large volumes of high-quality evidence to reschedule substances, while they accept small amounts of relatively low-quality, anecdotal evidence as sufficient to schedule drugs... These asymmetries create a one-way ratcheting effect...
We shouldn't have to suffer because a system is too scared to differentiate between treatment and abuse.

sections.executiveSummary: The central theme is that the Drug Enforcement Administration's (DEA) aggregate production quotas (APQs) for controlled substances are a primary cause of ongoing, critical shortages of ADHD medications and other essential drugs. Commenters from nearly all stakeholder groups, including patients, providers, and professional organizations, argue that the current quota system is inflexible, opaque, and based on flawed assumptions about diversion risk. The overwhelming sentiment is that the system prioritizes theoretical law enforcement goals over documented patient medical needs, leading to severe patient harm, destabilized care, and the rise of dangerous counterfeit drugs. The core recommendation is a fundamental reform of the quota-setting process to be more transparent, responsive to real-time medical demand, and centered on public health.

consensusPoints.text: DEA quotas are a primary driver of medication shortages. There is a widespread belief that the DEA's method of setting APQs is directly and artificially constraining the supply of necessary medications, particularly for ADHD, creating a public health crisis.

exceptions.text: The DEA, in its public statements and rules, maintains that quotas are set to meet estimated medical, scientific, and industrial needs while preventing diversion, and that shortages are often caused by manufacturers not producing up to their allocated quota.

consensusPoints.text: The current quota system lacks transparency and responsiveness. Commenters argue that the methodology for calculating quotas is a 'black box,' making it impossible for manufacturers and healthcare systems to plan. The system is seen as too rigid to adapt to changing patient needs or supply chain disruptions.

areasOfDebate.topic: Root Cause of Shortages: DEA Quotas vs. Broader Supply Chain Issues
areasOfDebate.description: While most agree quotas are a major factor, there is debate over whether they are the sole cause or one part of a larger, more complex problem involving manufacturers, distributors, and pharmacies.

positions.label: Quotas are Primary Cause
positions.stance: The vast majority of commenters, particularly patients and providers, position the DEA's quotas as the primary bottleneck. They argue that even if other issues exist, an adequate quota would allow the supply chain to meet demand. Organizations like the American Psychiatric Association and CHADD provided detailed accounts of how quota reductions directly preceded and exacerbated shortages.
positions.supportLevel: Vast majority
keyArguments: Shortages of specific drugs, like Vyvanse, intensified immediately following DEA announcements of quota levels that manufacturers had warned were insufficient.
The system does not adequately account for population growth, increased diagnosis rates, or shifts in prescribing patterns away from other substances.

positions.label: Broader Systemic Failures
positions.stance: A smaller but vocal group, including some pharmaceutical supply chain experts and the DEA itself, argues that blaming quotas alone is an oversimplification. This view, supported by organizations like the Healthcare Distribution Alliance, points to a multi-faceted problem.
positions.supportLevel: Significant minority
keyArguments: Manufacturers may not always produce the full amount of their allocated quota for business reasons, such as prioritizing more profitable drugs.
Distributors and pharmacies have their own controls and 'suspicious order' monitoring systems that can halt the flow of medication even when DEA quotas technically allow for more production.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The primary concern is the direct, severe, and negative impact on daily life and health. Commenters describe inability to work or attend school, severe withdrawal symptoms, emotional distress, and being forced to turn to illicit sources.
specificPoints: Parents describe the 'nightmare' of trying to find medication for their children, with one mother detailing the academic and emotional decline of her child after being unable to fill his prescription for three months.
Many express feelings of being treated like criminals for needing a legally prescribed medication, citing the stigma and hurdles created by the intense focus on diversion over patient care.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: This group is focused on the erosion of their ability to provide effective care and the immense administrative burden created by the shortages. Organizations like the American Medical Association and American Academy of Pediatrics submitted detailed comments.
specificPoints: Providers report spending hours each week trying to locate medications for patients, time that is not reimbursed and takes away from direct patient care. A psychiatrist noted this has become a major contributor to burnout.

Professional organizations argue for a fundamental shift in policy, recommending that the DEA be required to consult with HHS and patient advocacy groups to ensure quotas are based on public health data, not just law enforcement metrics.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The focus on diversion is disproportionate to the reality of prescribed use. The commenter argues that the entire system is structured around a small minority of potential bad actors, penalizing the vast majority of legitimate patients who rely on these medications to function.

A pharmacist noted that the shortages are creating a dangerous secondary market in counterfeit pills, often containing fentanyl. They argued that the DEA's quota policy, intended to reduce harm, is inadvertently fueling a more lethal crisis.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A recurring pattern is the comparison of the DEA's management of controlled substance quotas to other federal regulatory processes. Commenters from groups like the Healthcare Distribution Alliance and the American Psychiatric Association frequently contrast the DEA's opaque, unilateral decision-making with more collaborative and data-driven models used by the FDA or CMS, suggesting the DEA's approach is an outlier in federal health regulation.

keyQuotations.quote: "My child has been unable to get his prescribed ADHD medication for four months. His grades have plummeted, he is suffering from severe anxiety, and our family is in crisis. How can the DEA justify this? We are not criminals, we are patients."

"The DEA's APQ methodology is a black box that is actively harming our patients. It is a system built on outdated assumptions about diversion that fails to incorporate real-time clinical data. We call for an immediate, congressionally-mandated overhaul of the entire process."

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters, particularly from professional organizations and advocacy groups, provided well-reasoned arguments, often citing specific data points, patient experiences, and policy recommendations. The discourse was largely focused on systemic analysis rather than purely anecdotal complaints.

evidenceBase.explanation: Evidence consists of strong qualitative data (patient/provider testimony) and logical arguments from professional groups. However, there is a lack of hard, independent quantitative data to definitively model the impact of quotas vs. other factors, largely because the DEA's own data and methodology are not public.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: While manufacturers are discussed frequently, there are few direct comments from them in the public docket. Their perspective is often relayed secondhand through industry groups. This makes it difficult to fully assess their role in production decisions versus the constraints imposed by quotas.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High. The issue is complex, involving interactions between federal law enforcement policy (DEA), healthcare practice (prescribers), market dynamics (manufacturers, distributors), and patient needs. Understanding the problem requires knowledge of all these domains.

6.3.themeDescription: Patient Access Barriers for Controlled Substances

sections.executiveSummary: Public input on patient access to controlled substances reveals a system in crisis, described by patients as a source of constant anxiety, stigma, and life-disrupting barriers. An overwhelming consensus of patients with ADHD reports that chronic pharmacy shortages, inconsistent generic drug efficacy, and inflexible Schedule II regulations create a monthly "battle" that leads to treatment discontinuation and adverse health outcomes. While organizations focus on specific regulatory frictions, the dominant sentiment is that current policies, intended to prevent diversion, are disproportionately punishing and dehumanizing legitimate patients, with many arguing this desperation may inadvertently fuel illicit markets.

consensusPoints.text: Chronic pharmacy-level barriers make accessing ADHD medication a constant struggle. While this experience is nearly universal among patients, organizational commenters focus more on the upstream regulatory causes rather than the downstream pharmacy experience itself.

The inconsistent efficacy of different generic medications creates unpredictability and treatment failure. This is almost exclusively a patient-reported concern; no professional organizations or government bodies directly addressed bioequivalence issues at the pharmacy level.

Inflexible Schedule II regulations directly cause gaps in care. Organizations and policy experts also cite these rules but frame them as systemic issues, while patients describe their direct, personal consequences.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast majority of patients with ADHD

consensusPoints.text: Patients feel stigmatized and criminalized when seeking their prescribed medication.

exceptions.text: The National Association of Chain Drug Stores (NACDS) raises a related concern, noting that positive ID requirements for buprenorphine risk "further stigmatizing patients" and deterring them from seeking care.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Purpose and Effect of Restrictive Regulations

positions.label: Punitive Barriers
positions.stance: Current regulations (Schedule II rules, DEA quotas) primarily harm and punish legitimate patients without effectively addressing diversion.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly all individual patients, some policy advocates.
keyArguments: The system creates artificial shortages, forces patients into treatment gaps that derail their lives, and the immense emotional toll and stigma are a form of punishment. The difficulty of legal access may actually fuel a "diversion of desperation."

positions.label: Necessary Safeguards
positions.stance: Regulations are necessary to prevent diversion and abuse, but their implementation is flawed and creates unintended negative consequences.
positions.supportLevel: Most professional organizations, trade associations, and government entities.
keyArguments: The goal of preventing abuse is valid, but specific rules are impractical, overly broad, or have a "chilling effect" on legitimate care. The problem is not the existence of control, but the specific, poorly designed mechanisms of control.

positions.label: Exempt Buprenorphine
positions.stance: Including buprenorphine in SOR requirements creates a chilling effect that dangerously restricts access to a life-saving OUD treatment.
positions.supportLevel: Policy experts, citing the American Medical Association (AMA) and a bipartisan group of Senators.
keyArguments: The "absence of quantitative thresholds" for reporting makes distributors and pharmacies fearful of DEA action, causing them to limit buprenorphine orders. This directly contradicts the public health goal of expanding MOUD access.

positions.label: Maintain Oversight
positions.stance: The DEA's proposed rule and guidance suggest the agency believes SOR for buprenorphine is a necessary tool to monitor for and prevent diversion.
positions.supportLevel: This is the implicit position of the regulatory body that commenters are reacting against.
keyArguments: As a controlled substance, buprenorphine is subject to diversion risk, and monitoring systems are a key part of the DEA's mandate to prevent it.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The constant, severe anxiety caused by monthly pharmacy 'roulette'; chronic stock shortages; inconsistent efficacy of generic medications; and the inflexible Schedule II rules that prevent early refills or transfers.
specificPoints: The profound and visceral human cost of these barriers. They describe lives, careers, and families "falling apart," "drowning," and being "derailed" by treatment gaps. They uniquely articulate the emotional toll of being "treated like a criminal" for managing a medical condition.
Loosen Schedule II restrictions, ensure generic consistency, address shortages, and fundamentally shift the system from one of suspicion to one of care.
A teacher taking unpaid days off because she couldn't function without her medication.
An HVAC installer making dangerous mistakes at work after his pharmacy ran out of stock.
A mother whose lack of medication access led her to "completely fall apart."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Impractical or conflicting regulations that create administrative burden, stifle innovation (telehealth), and impede professionals' ability to provide care.
specificPoints: A focus on specific, technical points of friction in the law. For example, the Alliance for Pharmacy Compounding (APC) details how DEA's interpretation of "constructive transfer" and "anticipatory compounding" creates impossible situations for pharmacists. NACDS highlights the impracticality of patient ID requirements at the pharmacy counter.
Targeted, specific regulatory fixes: clarifying definitions, rescinding proposed rules, harmonizing state PDMPs, and exempting certain practices or drugs from specific requirements.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The direct causal link between regulatory-driven medication shortages and the rise of a deadly counterfeit drug market. The paradoxical effect of regulations creating the very public health crisis (overdoses from fake pills) they were designed to prevent.
specificPoints: They provide a data-driven, macro-level view of the problem, citing DEA seizure statistics and CDC warnings to connect policy choices to fatal outcomes.
Eliminate DEA production quotas that create artificial shortages and fund task forces to combat cartels exploiting these shortages.
Citing DEA data that counterfeit Adderall® pills containing fentanyl have surged and are linked to a 400% rise in overdose deaths.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Federal regulations (21 CFR 1306.07(a)) that severely limit methadone access for OUD to OTPs, preventing treatment in more accessible office-based settings.
specificPoints: A state-level view on how federal rules hamstring their ability to combat the opioid crisis, contrasting the US approach with more effective models, like Canada's.
Eliminate the restriction on office-based methadone prescribing and prohibit OTPs from denying care based on non-clinical barriers like lack of ID.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Several patients re-framed the issue of diversion, arguing that the system's extreme difficulty makes legal access so untenable that legitimate patients may be "forced to find 'workarounds'" or "go off script" out of sheer desperation to avoid a debilitating relapse. "Maybe the diversion problem isn’t greed, but desperation".

The Alliance for Pharmacy Compounding provided a highly technical but critical insight: DEA and FDA regulations are in conflict regarding compounding and delivery of controlled substances, creating a legal gray area that prevents patients from receiving medically necessary treatments like sterile injectables administered in a doctor's office.

Commenters whose jobs or lives involve travel (a truck driver, a military spouse) highlighted a critical flaw in the system. The inability to transfer Schedule II prescriptions across state lines or use more than one pharmacy creates a recurring crisis for a mobile population, effectively punishing them for their circumstances.

Commenters whose jobs or lives involve travel (a truck driver, a military spouse) highlighted a critical flaw in the system. The inability to transfer Schedule II prescriptions across state lines or use more than one pharmacy creates a recurring crisis for a mobile population, effectively punishing them for their circumstances.

The most alarming data-backed insight was the direct link made between DEA production quotas, the resulting shortages for legitimate patients, and the explosion of deadly, fentanyl-laced counterfeit Adderall on the illicit market, suggesting current policy is having the opposite of its intended effect.

The most alarming data-backed insight was the direct link made between DEA production quotas, the resulting shortages for legitimate patients, and the explosion of deadly, fentanyl-laced counterfeit Adderall on the illicit market, suggesting current policy is having the opposite of its intended effect.

The most alarming data-backed insight was the direct link made between DEA production quotas, the resulting shortages for legitimate patients, and the explosion of deadly, fentanyl-laced counterfeit Adderall on the illicit market, suggesting current policy is having the opposite of its intended effect.

emergingPatterns.pattern: There is a stark divide between the perspectives of patients and organizations. Patients provide raw, emotional, and harrowing accounts of the human cost of access barriers, conveying extreme urgency. Organizations provide more dispassionate, systemic analyses focused on regulatory mechanics.

A clear pattern emerges from dozens of comments: the more tightly the system controls access to prevent abuse by a few, the more it harms, destabilizes, and pushes the compliant majority toward desperation. "Because a tiny fraction of people might abuse stimulants, 100% of ADHD patients are made to suffer".

Access issues are exacerbated for those in rural areas (fewer pharmacies/prescribers), those living near state borders (facing licensing and PDMP issues), and those with mobile professions (truck drivers unable to use a single home pharmacy).

The problem of inconsistent generic efficacy is not an isolated issue but a widespread, recurring pattern that undermines the very foundation of treatment for a significant number of patients, turning every refill into an unpredictable "gamble."

keyQuotations.quote: [Every] single month, it’s a battle just to get my medication.
Every refill feels like a gamble.
This isn’t healthcare; it’s roulette with my well-being.
The lead-up to calling the pharmacy is straight-up anxiety. Like, full-body tension.
I’ve had to cancel events because I ran out of meds and couldn’t think clearly enough to coordinate.
When I miss a dose or run out because the pharmacy doesn't have it, I feel it immediately. It's like my brain short-circuits.
I’ve never felt more like a suspect than when trying to fill a legitimate prescription.
If this were blood pressure meds or insulin, people would be furious. But for ADHD? We get treated like we’re gaming the system.
Why is the entire system built around stopping a few people from misusing, instead of helping the rest of us survive? We are real patients. And yet every policy treats us like suspects.
Stop penalizing patients for trying to stay well.
Primary Care Physicians (PCPs) often do not want to prescribe stimulants. The DEA’s regulations make PCPs feel like they will be criminalized for helping patients.
The human cost of maintaining the status quo is measured not just in medication shortages, but in the lives endangered by the counterfeit alternatives that current policies have made inevitable.
If people are diverting meds, maybe it’s not greed; it’s because the system makes legal access nearly impossible.
Because a tiny fraction of people might abuse stimulants, 100% of ADHD patients are made to suffer.
It’s wild that in the most technologically advanced country in the world, I can’t reliably get medication that’s been around since World War II.
Without formal guidance from the DEA, the absence of quantitative thresholds has created chilling effects on access to buprenorphine...
We need fewer restrictions that make it harder, not easier, to get these medications.
What we need is consistent, reliable access to care – not endless barriers, suspicion, and stigma.
And please stop treating us like addicts. We’re patients, not abusers.
We’re not trying to scam the system; we are being scammed by it.

6.4.themeDescription: Drug and Biologic Approval Processes. This sub-theme covers the regulatory pathways for bringing new drugs and biologics to market. || It includes concerns about the speed and efficiency of FDA reviews, user fee waiver policies for small companies, the requirements of specific pathways like Accelerated Approval, and the need for greater flexibility for advanced therapies (cell and gene) and rare disease drugs. It also covers calls to reduce reliance on animal testing by accepting New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on drug and biologic approval processes is overwhelmingly unified in its call for significant modernization, efficiency, and flexibility. A powerful consensus exists across industry, patient advocates, and providers that current regulatory frameworks are too slow, rigid, and laden with outdated requirements that stifle innovation, increase costs, and delay patient access to therapies. The core tension lies in balancing the demand for accelerated, risk-based pathways—especially for advanced therapies and rare diseases—against entrenched, one-size-fits-all review standards and processes. Dominant recommendations urge the FDA to repeal specific redundant regulations, harmonize with modern scientific and international standards, and embrace greater flexibility through the adoption of Real-World Evidence and New Approach Methodologies.

consensusPoints.text: The FDA approval process is too slow, inefficient, and burdensome. A taxpayer advocacy group notes it takes "more than ten years and costs over $2 billion" to bring a drug to market. A biopharmaceutical company states that current regulations "unlawfully delay the drug approval process," with average approval times far exceeding the statutory 180 days. An academic physician criticizes the pipeline as "slow, inflexible, and heavily reliant on expensive, time-consuming traditional randomized controlled trials".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast majority of commenters, particularly from industry, trade associations, and patient advocacy groups

exceptions.text: While the desire for speed is nearly universal, some commenters imply a need to balance speed with safety. For example, pharmacist groups' opposition to the ACNU rule is based on patient safety concerns with accelerated access, and one provider group opposes removing printed drug information for similar safety reasons.

consensusPoints.text: FDA regulations contain numerous outdated, redundant, and scientifically unnecessary requirements. PhRMA and BIO submit detailed catalogs of regulations to be rescinded, such as those requiring rabbit pyrogen testing (21 CFR 610.13(b)) or submission of physical samples and paper case report forms (21 CFR 314.50(e), 314.50(f)(2)), which are now superseded by modern technology. A regulatory consulting firm highlights the redundancy of the Integrated Summary of Effectiveness (ISE) requirement, which duplicates information found elsewhere in submissions.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all industry and trade association commenters

exceptions.text: No commenters explicitly defended the specific outdated regulations cited by industry.
