stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Hospitals & Health Systems (Mayo, Cleveland Clinic, AAMC, GNYHA, Select Medical, etc.)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Administrative inefficiency, care delays, and provider burnout caused by documentation and supervision rules.
specificPoints: Focus on the operational friction of these rules. They describe 'weeks-long delays' in care and therapists spending excessive administrative time trying to get physician signatures on therapy plans.
Make virtual supervision permanent, streamline documentation for split/shared visits, and grant more flexibility for interdisciplinary teams (e.g., allow more staff types to develop care plans).
Select Medical highlights the absurdity of non-payment for medically necessary services 'solely due to difficulty in obtaining signatures' from overworked primary care physicians.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Physician Groups & Individuals (ASA, PAI, Dr. Chustecki, Dr. Younglove)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vary widely, from patient safety (ASA) to administrative burden (Dr. Chustecki) to practice viability (Dr. Younglove).
specificPoints: The ASA provides the sole, forceful defense of supervision for a specific service line (anesthesia). Dr. Younglove provides a compelling account of how 'incident to' rules harm small, independent practices by making it impossible to afford and utilize part-time auxiliary staff, favoring large, integrated systems.
Range from maintaining the status quo (ASA) to deregulation and making flexibilities permanent (PAI, Dr. Chustecki).
The ASA directly refutes claims that medical direction is more expensive, explaining that the anesthesiologist and CRNA each bill for 50% of the total, resulting in a 100% payment to the care team.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial viability of pharmacist integration into care teams.
specificPoints: APhA does not want to eliminate 'incident to' billing but reform it. They argue that restricting them to billing the lowest-level code (99211) for complex medication management services 'undervalues pharmacists' and makes the model unsustainable.
Allow physicians to bill for pharmacists' E/M services at higher complexity levels (99212-215) under 'incident to' rules, consistent with many state and commercial plans.
A detailed case study of a 42-minute visit with a complex patient, which is far beyond the 7-minute estimate for a 99211 code.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The American Academy of Physician Associates (AAPA) offers a unique warning: broadly extending virtual supervision to billing providers like PAs could inadvertently expand the use of 'detrimental' 'incident to' billing, further hiding their work in claims data. They propose a strategic alternative: limit it to supervising non-billing staff like RNs and pharmacists to expand access without harming data transparency.

Dr. Courtney Younglove provides a compelling case that 'incident to' rules requiring in-office physician presence disproportionately harm small, independent practices. Unlike large health systems that can afford full-time dietitians and other staff, small clinics that rely on shared, part-time professionals cannot meet the supervision requirements, thus penalizing innovation and team-based care where it may be most needed.

The American Pharmacists Association (APhA) presents a data-backed argument not to repeal 'incident to' but to reform it. They show how their complex, 40+ minute medication management consultations are being forced into a 7-minute, low-value billing code (99211), making the model financially non-viable and preventing their full integration into care teams.

The American Academy of Ambulatory Care Nursing (AAACN) powerfully reframes the role of nurses, stating, 'The work that RNs do is not incidental to physician work, it is instrumental to patient care.' This crystallizes the core objection to the 'incident to' framework: it fundamentally devalues and mischaracterizes the work of the nursing profession.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Across nearly all stakeholder groups (NPPs, hospitals, rural health advocates, telehealth groups), the successful implementation of supervision waivers during the COVID-19 PHE is used as the primary evidence that permanent reform is safe, effective, and long overdue. Commenters repeatedly state there was 'no demonstrable negative effect on patient care.'

The analysis reveals that supervision rules create unique pain points depending on the care setting. Commenters identified specific regulatory burdens in Critical Access Hospitals (co-signatures), Skilled Nursing Facilities (initial visit restrictions), outpatient clinics (on-site presence), and home health (per-patient aide supervision), indicating a need for tailored, setting-specific reforms.

The negative impact of strict, on-site supervision is magnified in rural areas. Commenters like the NRHA and ANA emphasize that workforce shortages and geographic distance make these rules not just burdensome but often impossible to follow, creating significant access-to-care deserts.

keyQuotations.quote: [Incident to billing] adds insult to injury by perpetuating an 'Incident to' billing policy that recognizes the billing provider [deemed to be the physician in most institutional settings], not the treating provider who would be paid only 85% of the physician’s charges].
The work that RNs do is not incidental to physician work, it is instrumental to patient care.
Primary care physicians are overworked; APTA reports it can take months and dozens of attempts to obtain signatures.
“Incident to” billing… is a massive roadblock for APRNs looking to start their own business, as they would be reimbursed less for the same work.

The erroneous claim that anesthesia payments under medical direction or supervision are more expensive misrepresents Medicare payment processes and is aimed at confusing policymakers on anesthesia billing and payment regulations.
Co-signature is an unnecessary administrative burden on physicians, diverting time from patient care without improving quality.
[The current framework] undervalues pharmacists, conflicts with state reimbursement, impedes access, and interferes with chronic disease management efforts.”
Federal regulations governing supervision 'often override state-level advancements in provider autonomy,' restricting team-based chronic care models.
Many of these requirements were relaxed by the Trump Administration during the COVID-19 public health emergency (PHE) with no demonstrable negative effect on patient care.
It is 'illogical' to require highly compensated staff (physicians) to supervise non-licensed staff in community settings.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on staffing standards is overwhelmingly critical of rigid, one-size-fits-all mandates, particularly the new nursing home minimum staffing rule. A near-unanimous consensus among healthcare providers and their associations is that such regulations are unworkable and dangerous amidst a severe, national workforce crisis. The core tension is between the regulatory goal of improving quality through mandated inputs versus the on-the-ground reality where these mandates are seen as financially unsustainable, likely to reduce access to care through facility closures, and not guaranteed to improve outcomes. Key recommendations focus on repealing these mandates in favor of flexible, facility-led staffing plans, significant investment in workforce development, and a shift toward outcome-based quality measures.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, particularly those representing healthcare facilities, argue that rigid staffing ratios or hours-per-day requirements fail to account for critical variables like patient acuity, facility size, geography, and available technology. They contend that facilities themselves are best positioned to determine appropriate staffing levels. For example, PAR noted, "Every LTC facility is different, and a one-size-fits-all approach is not the best solution for residents," a view shared by LeadingAge PA. The Federation of American Hospitals "strongly opposes any move toward implementing specific nurse-to-patient ratios in regulation or sub-regulatory guidance," arguing they would "reduce flexibility."

exceptions.text: Commenters from the early childhood education sector (Head Start) like ZERO TO THREE and MiSide view minimum ratios and group sizes as essential for maintaining child health and safety.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters across every healthcare sector (hospitals, long-term care, pharmacy, dental) agree that a profound and persistent workforce shortage makes compliance with new, higher staffing standards impossible. They argue that the required personnel simply do not exist. For example, the American Hospital Association stated the nursing home rule is "unworkable due to severe workforce shortages." The Delta Dental Plans Association noted that dental appointment wait times are determined by "widespread provider shortages...most pronounced in rural and other underserved areas." PPLA calculated that a new pharmacy rule would require the equivalent of 35,858 full-time pharmacy technicians, creating "a shortage of pharmacy technicians."

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to repeal or maintain mandatory minimum staffing standards, pitting providers who see them as unworkable against advocates who view them as essential for safety.

positions.label: Rescind Mandates
positions.stance: Repeal prescriptive federal staffing requirements and grant facilities flexibility.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, including all hospital, long-term care, and provider associations (AHA, FAH, AHCA/NCAL, Select Medical, LeadingAge PA, PAR, etc.).
keyArguments: Impossibility: Severe workforce shortages make hiring targets (e.g., 102,000 additional LTC staff) unattainable.
Negative Consequences: Will force facility closures, displace residents, and reduce access to care, especially in rural areas.
Ineffectiveness: Mandates are unfunded, do not account for patient acuity, and do not necessarily improve care quality.

positions.label: Support Ratios
positions.stance: Maintain minimum staffing standards to protect vulnerable populations.
positions.supportLevel: A small minority in this sample, exclusively from the early childhood education sector.
keyArguments: Safety & Quality: Minimum ratios and group sizes are essential to "help maintain children’s health and safety."
Improved Interaction: They maximize opportunities for children to interact with teachers.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The nursing home staffing rule will have negative downstream effects on hospitals (e.g., difficulty discharging patients). CMS interpretive guidance for hospitals is creating de facto staffing ratios, exceeding statutory authority.
specificPoints: They see the nursing home rule as a dangerous precedent that could be applied to hospitals next. They also highlight the unique burden on Critical Access Hospitals (CAHs) from duplicative federal and state staffing rules. The Federation of American Hospitals is concerned that CMS guidance is moving toward rigid ratios not authorized by law.

Proposed solutions include repealing the nursing home rule, rescinding CMS interpretive guidance implying hospital ratios, and deferring to state authority for CAH staffing. Ascension argues federal CAH staffing rules are duplicative of state licensure and should be eliminated.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The nursing home staffing rule is an existential threat due to unachievable hiring requirements, massive unfunded costs, and the high risk of facility closures. Flawed data collection rules (PBJ) distort staffing levels and create immense administrative burdens.
specificPoints: This group provides the most detailed critique of the operational impossibilities, citing specific costs ($43 billion over 10 years), hiring deficits (102,000 staff), and flawed reporting mechanics (PBJ meal break deductions, exclusion of telehealth). Select Medical notes only 18% of facilities would have met the rule in 2023-24.

Proposed solutions include repealing the rule entirely, overhauling the Payroll-Based Journal (PBJ) reporting system, and focusing on workforce development and outcome-based measures instead of input mandates. AHCA/NCAL provides a detailed list of PBJ reporting flaws that disincentivize innovation and misrepresent care hours.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Staffing shortages in their respective fields (pharmacy, dentistry) make compliance with other federal regulations (drug information dispensing, appointment wait times) unrealistic.
specificPoints: These commenters illustrate the ripple effect of the national workforce crisis, showing how it impacts regulations beyond direct care ratios.

They propose to repeal or revise regulations that do not account for the reality of provider shortages. PPLA calculates a proposed rule would create a 71.7 million-hour workload increase for pharmacies, while Delta Dental argues it is unfair to hold them to wait time standards when provider shortages are the root cause.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining standards that ensure child safety and positive developmental interactions.
specificPoints: This group offers the only support for staffing ratios in the provided comments, viewing them as a foundational element of quality. As ZERO TO THREE states, minimum ratios 'help maintain children’s health and safety.' However, they also identify the practical challenge of implementation, as MiSide points out the tension when one child's needs stretch the remaining staff too thin.
They propose to maintain existing ratio standards for Head Start programs.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Data Is Flawed by Design: AHCA/NCAL provides a crucial insight that the current Payroll-Based Journal (PBJ) data system—the very system used to measure compliance—is inherently inaccurate. Rules that exclude telehealth hours, mandate 30-minute meal deductions even if staff work through them, and misrepresent salaried employee hours actively distort the data, punishing innovation and providing a false picture of staffing.

Ratios Are Not a Universal Solution: The stark contrast between the Head Start commenters, who see ratios as essential for safety, and the healthcare providers, who see them as a threat to access, highlights that the utility and feasibility of staffing mandates are highly context-dependent.

Staffing Shortages Create Domino Effects: Comments from PPLA and Delta Dental demonstrate that the workforce crisis is a systemic issue. A regulation in one area (e.g., patient information) can become impossible to implement due to labor shortages in another (pharmacy), showing the interconnectedness of regulatory burdens and workforce capacity.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Commenters like NRHA, FAH, and Delta Dental consistently identify rural areas as disproportionately harmed by rigid staffing mandates. Due to more acute and persistent provider shortages, rural facilities (hospitals, SNFs, dental offices) would be the first to be unable to comply, likely leading to closures and creating care deserts.

Facility-Type Patterns: Opposition to mandated ratios is monolithic in acute and post-acute care settings (hospitals, LTC facilities). In contrast, support for ratios is strong in early childhood education settings, suggesting the perceived value and practicality differ dramatically by the population served and care model.

Unintended Consequences: A clear pattern emerges of commenters predicting that the regulations will achieve the opposite of their intent. Instead of improving quality, they will force reliance on costly, lower-quality temporary agency staff (as argued by PAR); shrink the future workforce by penalizing facilities and removing their ability to host CNA training programs (a concern from AHCA/NCAL); and decrease, rather than increase, access to care for the most vulnerable populations by forcing closures (a warning from Select Medical).

keyQuotations.quote: [The nursing home rule] will not necessarily guarantee higher quality care... If LTC facilities cannot find these additional workers, nearly one-quarter of residents may be at risk of displacement.
Without additional federal funding, this rule will exacerbate staffing issues and lead to further facility closures, diminishing access to care.

The imposition of nurse staffing ratios would 'severely burden hospitals struggling with nursing workforce shortages,' potentially leading to care delays or temporary closures of units, particularly in rural and underserved areas.
Every LTC facility is different, and a one-size-fits-all approach is not the best solution for residents.

Appointment wait times are not controlled by SADPs. They are determined mainly by widespread provider shortages (especially for dental specialists) that are most pronounced in rural and other underserved areas, and by how well-staffed dental offices are.
The regulation would increase dispenser workloads by 71.7 million hours, the equivalent of 35,858 full-time pharmacy technicians.

Current PBJ guidance states only paid hours should be reported, meaning if a salaried employee works 10 hours but is paid for 8, only 8 are reported... impacting accuracy for nearly all exempt employee job classifications and resulting in inaccurate RN and total nursing staffing hours...

CMS should shift the focus from fixed staffing requirements and process indicators to quality outcomes to allow LTC providers the regulatory flexibility to adjust staffing and programming to best fit the needs of current residents.
Minimum ratios and group sizes for Head Start and Early Head Start programs help maintain children’s health and safety while maximizing opportunities for children to interact with teachers.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment overwhelmingly supports significant GME reform to address critical physician workforce shortages, particularly in primary care and rural areas. A powerful consensus exists for making virtual supervision of residents permanent and for expanding the number of Medicare-funded residency slots. Key tensions revolve around how to allocate new slots—whether broadly or targeted to specific needs—and the scope of modern medical training. Commenters raise urgent concerns about systemic barriers, most notably alleged discrimination against osteopathic physicians (DOs) in residency selection and regulatory hurdles that stifle the growth of rural training programs.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of institutional commenters, including major health systems and associations, advocate for making permanent the pandemic-era flexibility allowing teaching physicians to supervise residents virtually. They argue it is a proven, safe, and effective tool that expands training opportunities, especially for telehealth, and reflects modern medical practice. For instance, the AAMC strongly supports revising regulations to permanently allow virtual supervision of residents for both in-person and telehealth services in all residency training locations where safe and effective. Mayo Clinic recommends harmonizing supervision rules to permit the same degree of virtual supervision for trainees as for clinical staff, noting the current policy's 2025 expiration limits the development of trainee capability. The AMA notes the successful employment of permanent audio/visual supervision in recent years and urges that it be made permanent.

exceptions.text: Support is nearly universal among those who mention it, with no opposing views presented. Commenters like the AAMC add the condition that it must be "safe and effective."

consensusPoints.text: Most stakeholders agree that the current number of Medicare-funded GME slots is insufficient to meet national health needs. There is broad support for lifting the cap and/or reforming allocation to expand the physician pipeline, seen as essential for addressing shortages in primary care, rural/underserved areas, and specific specialties. For example, MaineHealth states plainly, "Removal of the cap will help meet the rising need for physicians, especially in rural areas." The American Academy of Hospice and Palliative Medicine (AAHPM) links specialist shortages directly to GME policy, stating the problem "largely stems from policies limiting Medicare-funding of residency slots" and calls for expansion. Dr. Margaret Chustecki of Yale School of Medicine argues current GME policy "does not sufficiently incentivize primary care training" and calls for expanding primary care slots.

exceptions.text: While no one argued against expansion, there is debate on the method of expansion. MaineHealth provides a key caveat, noting that even recent expansions under the Consolidated Appropriation Act of 2023 excluded them due to flawed prioritization criteria that favored states with new medical schools.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to broadly lift the cap on GME slots for all teaching hospitals or to reform allocation to specifically target areas of greatest need, such as primary care and rural communities.

positions.label: Broad Expansion
positions.stance: Lift the existing cap on GME slots for teaching hospitals to broadly increase physician supply. This position is supported by commenters such as MaineHealth.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily large healthcare systems.
keyArguments: A rising tide lifts all boats; a simple cap removal is the most direct way to meet rising demand, especially in areas with established training infrastructure.
Current targeted criteria can be exclusionary and counterproductive.

positions.label: Targeted Prioritization
positions.stance: Reform allocation to specifically direct new slots to areas of greatest need, such as primary care, rural communities, and shortage specialties. This position is supported by advocacy groups and specialty organizations like AACOM and AAHPM, and academics like Dr. Margaret Chustecki.
positions.supportLevel: Most advocacy groups and specialty organizations.
keyArguments: A general increase won't necessarily fix maldistribution. Funding must be intentionally directed to correct existing imbalances in geography and specialty.
Current prioritization methods (like HPSA scores) are flawed and need refinement, not abandonment.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether GME should maintain its traditional clinical focus or be expanded to include modern skills like data science, nutrition, and behavioral science.

positions.label: Traditional Focus
positions.stance: An implicit position held by most commenters, such as the AMA, AAMC, and Mayo Clinic, focusing on reforming the structure and funding of GME without detailing curriculum changes.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of commenters focus on funding, slots, and supervision.
keyArguments: The most pressing issues are access to and financing of training, not the core content.

positions.label: Expanded Curriculum
positions.stance: Require residency programs to incorporate non-traditional but critical modern skills. This position is articulated by organizations like Healthcare Actually and the American College of Lifestyle Medicine.
positions.supportLevel: A small but distinct minority of forward-looking organizations.
keyArguments: To meet modern health challenges, physicians need skills beyond traditional anatomy and pharmacology.
Competency in nutrition, data analysis, and behavioral science is essential for addressing the chronic disease epidemic.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Systemic policy flaws, including the GME cap, discriminatory practices against DOs, lack of training parity for PAs/NPs, and regulatory barriers to rural training.
specificPoints: These groups provide the most detailed policy analysis and data. AACOM, for example, offers specific statistics on DO exclusion and the counterintuitive negative impacts of single accreditation.
NRHA uniquely identifies the chilling effect of Stark/Anti-kickback laws on rural preceptorships.

Proposed solutions include comprehensive regulatory and legislative reforms—making virtual supervision permanent (AMA, AAMC), removing the GME cap (AAHPM), establishing anti-discrimination rules for DOs (AACOM), and creating safe harbors for rural training partnerships (NRHA).
AACOM's detailed exposé on barriers facing osteopathic physicians, including data showing 73% of programs require DOs to take the USMLE, up from 56% in 2022.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Operational efficiency, workforce pipeline, and removing barriers to collaboration. They are focused on the practical implementation of GME programs.
specificPoints: They highlight the real-world impact of specific regulations. MaineHealth provides a concrete example of being excluded from new GME slots by flawed federal criteria.
Ascension points to specific CMS rules on 'director control' that inhibit hospital-university partnerships for allied health training.

Proposed solutions include pragmatic regulatory changes like making virtual supervision permanent (Mayo Clinic, Cleveland Clinic), removing the GME cap (MaineHealth), and relaxing rules on allied health education payments to foster collaboration (Ascension).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient access to care and the real-world consequences of physician shortages.
specificPoints: These comments provide a powerful, ground-level view of system failure. The anonymous mother's story of struggling to find a psychiatrist who treats adult ADHD, facing 6-month waitlists, and encountering PCPs unwilling to prescribe medication gives a human face to the statistics on workforce shortages.

Proposed solutions are focused on the outcome: "Help train more providers to treat ADHD". Dr. Chustecki, a practicing physician, similarly calls for expanding primary care slots based on direct clinical experience.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The American Association of Colleges of Osteopathic Medicine (AACOM) makes the striking observation that since the transition to a single GME accreditation system, the percentage of residency programs requiring DOs to take the separate MD licensure exam (USMLE) has actually increased from 56% to 73%. This suggests the policy, intended to create parity, may have had the opposite effect in practice.

The anonymous comment from a mother with ADHD provides a visceral, human-scale illustration of what a "specialist shortage" means: "6-month waitlists only to be told by providers 'I don’t handle that.'" It powerfully connects abstract policy debates to tangible human suffering.

The National Rural Health Association (NRHA) identifies a specific, non-obvious barrier to expanding the rural physician pipeline: "legal ambiguity around compensation for teaching" and fear of violating Stark and Anti-kickback Laws prevents rural hospitals from partnering with universities to precept learners.

Healthcare Actually offers a forward-looking, disruptive recommendation to "require residencies to graduate doctors fluent in SQL, behavior change science, and climate-resilient supply chains alongside anatomy." This reframes the GME debate beyond just slots and funding to the fundamental skill set required for modern medicine.

emergingPatterns.pattern: There is a clear and persistent emphasis on the unique challenges facing rural communities. Commenters like the NRHA, MaineHealth, and AACOM highlight how national "one-size-fits-all" GME policies often fail or even harm rural areas, whether through flawed allocation criteria or by creating legal risks for training partnerships.

Multiple commenters identify how well-intentioned policies have had negative knock-on effects. The exclusion of MaineHealth from new GME slots, the increase in USMLE requirements for DOs post-single accreditation, and the potential for HPSA scores to disqualify rural programs from receiving residents all point to a pattern of policies whose real-world impacts diverge from their stated goals.

While most of the focus is on physicians, several key comments advocate for a broader view. The AAPA argues for training parity for Physician Associates, the NNPEN calls for an NP education loan program, and Ascension focuses on barriers to training allied health professionals, suggesting a growing recognition that solving the workforce crisis requires a multi-professional approach.

keyQuotations.quote: [F]ederally-funded Medicare GME programs should not be allowed to discriminate against a class of physicians based solely on degree and exam type.

MaineHealth was excluded from receiving any new residency slots because we did not meet any of the criteria, including the category that prioritized states with new medical schools. Removal of the cap will help meet the rising need for physicians, especially in rural areas.

I have called local psychiatrists and most are not accepting new patients. Some have on their websites 'we don’t treat ADHD.' ... This would eliminate issues like 6 month wait lists only to be told by providers 'I don’t handle that.'
There is no medical basis for these policies, as DO and MD degrees and both medical exams lead to unrestricted physician licenses in all 50 states.

Rural hospitals and clinics often hesitate to form precepting or stipend partnerships with universities due to legal ambiguity around compensation for teaching, fearing regulatory violations under Stark and Anti-kickback Laws.

Ascension also suggests reforming nursing and allied health education payments by...eliminating the legal operator requirement (42 C.F.R 413.85(f)), which would enhance collaborations between hospitals and educational institutions, bettering access to specialized expertise and resources.

Medical school is highly demanding and intensive, and osteopathic medical students should not be subjected to the additional 32 hours and $2,335 (as well as prep costs and time) that are required to take the USMLE, an exam that is not designed for the osteopathic profession or needed for licensure or practice.
[W]e recommend requiring residencies to graduate doctors fluent in SQL, behavior change science, and climate-resilient supply chains alongside anatomy.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on provider non-compete agreements is sharply polarized between clinical labor and employer organizations. All nursing groups unanimously advocate for a ban on these agreements for clinicians in Medicare/Medicaid facilities, arguing they unjustly suppress wages, limit mobility, and worsen workforce shortages. Conversely, hospital and payer associations call for the repeal of federal bans on non-competes, viewing them as necessary tools to protect intellectual property and prevent unfair competition.

consensusPoints.text: All four nursing organizations (ANA, AACN, AWHONN) are in complete agreement that non-compete agreements have a detrimental effect on nurses and the broader healthcare system. They argue these agreements are not justified for frontline clinicians, who do not possess proprietary corporate secrets, and instead serve to suppress wages, limit job mobility, and worsen workforce shortages. They consistently assert that existing protections like HIPAA and non-disclosure agreements are sufficient to protect patient and business information.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All four nursing organizations are in complete agreement

exceptions.text: This view is not shared by commenters representing employers and payers. The American Hospital Association (AHA) and UnitedHealth Group (UHG) oppose the federal rule banning non-competes, indicating they see value and legitimacy in these agreements.

areasOfDebate.topic: Legitimacy and Impact of Non-Compete Agreements in Healthcare
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether non-compete agreements for clinicians are an exploitative labor practice to be banned or a legitimate business protection tool to be preserved.

positions.label: Ban Agreements
positions.stance: Prohibit non-compete agreements for clinicians, particularly nurses, as an exploitative labor practice.
positions.supportLevel: All four commenters representing nursing organizations (ANA, AACN, AWHONN).
keyArguments: Wage Suppression: Agreements take nurses "out of the free market," artificially capping salaries and preventing them from seeking higher-paying jobs.
No Legitimate Business Need: Frontline nurses do not have access to the kind of corporate trade secrets that non-competes are designed to protect; HIPAA and NDAs already cover sensitive information.

Exacerbates Shortages: By "tethering" nurses to specific employers and limiting their mobility, these agreements discourage participation in the workforce and can force clinicians to leave the field or a specialty entirely.

positions.label: Protect Agreements
positions.stance: Preserve the ability of employers to use non-compete agreements and repeal federal rules that ban them.
positions.supportLevel: Both commenters representing employer and payer organizations (AHA, UHG).
keyArguments: Protect Intellectual Property: Non-competes are necessary tools to inhibit the loss of intellectual property.
Prevent Unfair Competition: The agreements are needed to prevent unfair competition between organizations.
Regulatory Overreach: The Federal Trade Commission's Non-Compete Clause Final Rule goes beyond the agency's statutory authority.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Suppressed wages for nurses, restricted career mobility, and the exacerbation of the national nursing shortage.
specificPoints: They consistently emphasize that the rationale for non-competes in other industries (protecting trade secrets) does not apply to frontline clinicians. They frame the issue as one of labor rights and professional autonomy, arguing the agreements are purely punitive and coercive.
A federal ban on the use of non-compete agreements for nurses in any facility that accepts Medicare and Medicaid.

The ANA and AWHONN both cite an extreme case of a non-compete with a 7,500-mile geographic restriction, illustrating how these agreements can be used to trap employees rather than protect legitimate, local business interests.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory overreach by the FTC, the ability to protect intellectual property, and maintaining a competitive business environment.
specificPoints: These stakeholders view non-competes through a business and legal lens, focusing on market dynamics and proprietary information rather than labor conditions. UHG specifically mentions the rule enables "unfair competition" by not applying to non-profits.
Full repeal of the FTC's Non-Compete Clause Rule.
UnitedHealth Group argues the rule "inhibits the protection of intellectual property".

noteworthyInsights.insight: The most articulate and consistent insight from nursing advocates is the distinction between frontline clinicians and other professionals. They argue that since a nurse’s value is in their licensed skill set, not proprietary information, non-competes are inappropriate. This reframes the debate from a business protection issue to a labor rights issue.

The most articulate and consistent insight from nursing advocates is the distinction between frontline clinicians and other professionals. They argue that since a nurse’s value is in their licensed skill set, not proprietary information, non-competes are inappropriate. This reframes the debate from a business protection issue to a labor rights issue.

The specific anecdote of a 7,500-mile non-compete clause, cited by both the ANA and AWHONN, is a highly effective advocacy tool. It crystallizes the potential for abuse in a way that abstract arguments cannot, making the threat feel immediate and unreasonable.

The specific anecdote of a 7,500-mile non-compete clause, cited by both the ANA and AWHONN, is a highly effective advocacy tool. It crystallizes the potential for abuse in a way that abstract arguments cannot, making the threat feel immediate and unreasonable.

AWHONN connects the non-compete issue to a broader struggle for the nursing profession's self-regulation, stating, "AWHONN firmly believes healthcare professionals should regulate their respective professions, and other professions must not be primary in regulating nursing." This elevates the concern from a contract term to a matter of professional respect and governance.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Clear Stakeholder Polarization: The feedback reveals a stark, irreconcilable divide between clinical labor groups and employer/payer organizations. There is no middle ground presented; one side sees non-competes as an exploitative tool to be banned, while the other sees them as a legitimate business practice to be protected from federal overreach.

Focus on Nursing: All detailed arguments, both for and against, are centered on the nursing profession. While the theme is "Provider Non-Compete Agreements," the submitted comments primarily address the impact on RNs and APRNs.

Coordinated Advocacy: The identical phrasing and use of the same 7,500-mile example across multiple nursing organizations (ANA, AWHONN) suggest a coordinated and unified advocacy campaign on this issue.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] only reason for frontline nurses to sign non-compete agreements is to force them to stay in their current job, limit their employment opportunities, take them out of the free market, and artificially cap salaries.
One member mentioned that their office had a non-compete that forbade them from working as a nurse for a speciﬁed period of time within 7,500 miles of their current workplace.
Banning non-compete agreements for nurses would raise salaries by increasing competition for nurses who are in demand without decreasing the supply of nurses in the marketplace.
[The Non-Compete Clause Final Rule] goes beyond statutory authority, inhibits the protection of intellectual property, and enables unfair competition.

Some non-compete agreements...are extreme...[they] are not intended to protect business interests but to tether nurses to specific roles, reducing their employment prospects and exacerbating workforce shortages.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Association of Woman’s Health, Obstetrics, and Neonatal Nurses

keyQuotations.quote: AWHONN firmly believes healthcare professionals should regulate their respective professions, and other professions must not be primary in regulating nursing.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Association of Woman’s Health, Obstetrics, and Neonatal Nurses

sections.executiveSummary: An overwhelming consensus of stakeholders from across the healthcare ecosystem identifies excessive regulatory and administrative burden as the primary driver of a burnout crisis that threatens the stability of the entire U.S. healthcare workforce. Commenters universally agree that burdens from documentation, quality reporting, and prior authorization divert critical time and resources from patient care, leading to workforce shortages and high turnover. While there is broad agreement on the problem, a key tension exists between those advocating for targeted, specific reforms to the most egregious rules and those calling for a more fundamental overhaul of the nation's "punitive" oversight culture and unsustainable financial models.

consensusPoints.text: Administrative burden is the primary driver of a workforce burnout crisis.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually every commenter (at least 38 of 40) explicitly or implicitly linked regulatory and administrative burdens to a state of crisis-level burnout, professional dissatisfaction, and workforce shortages across the entire healthcare spectrum. This consensus is held by healthcare providers, trade associations, advocacy groups, and businesses alike.

exceptions.text: While the link is universally acknowledged, stakeholders emphasize different types of administrative burdens as the most egregious, ranging from EHR documentation and quality reporting to prior authorization and operational rules.

consensusPoints.text: Burnout directly diverts resources and time from patient care.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (over 30 of 40) framed the issue not just as a workforce problem, but as a direct threat to patient care. They argue that time and resources spent on compliance are finite and come at the expense of clinical activities.

consensusPoints.text: The burnout crisis extends beyond physicians and nurses.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A significant and diverse group of commenters (at least 15 of 40) made a point to highlight that burnout driven by regulatory and financial pressures is endemic across many healthcare and caring professions.

positions.label: Red Tape
positions.stance: The core problem is the sheer volume and inefficiency of administrative rules (documentation, reporting, PA).
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, particularly provider groups and trade associations.
keyArguments: Time spent on paperwork is time not spent with patients
specific regulations like MIPS, OASIS, and prior authorization are demonstrably burdensome with little clinical benefit
these tasks directly cause burnout and workforce exit.

positions.label: Financial Pressure
positions.stance: The core problem is inadequate compensation and financial unsustainability, which makes the administrative burden intolerable.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but vocal contingent, especially those in underfunded sectors like early childhood education and community-based services.
keyArguments: Low wages and reimbursement rates are the fundamental drivers of turnover
administrative tasks are more burdensome when there aren't resources to hire dedicated staff
financial penalties for non-compliance are punitive and counterproductive.

positions.label: Targeted Reform
positions.stance: Address the most burdensome regulations one by one with specific, commonsense fixes.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of commenters who offer concrete solutions.
keyArguments: A targeted approach is practical and can yield immediate relief
specific fixes include streamlining documentation, automating reporting, removing duplicative requirements, and clarifying rules
This allows for burden reduction without dismantling the entire regulatory structure.

positions.label: Systemic Overhaul
positions.stance: The system of oversight and reimbursement itself is broken and needs fundamental rethinking.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority, including some large associations and advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Piecemeal fixes are insufficient
the entire "punitive" oversight culture needs to change
payment models that don't value clinicians' time are inherently flawed
focusing on a different model of care (e.g., lifestyle medicine) could restore professional joy.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Cumulative administrative burden from multiple, overlapping federal programs; high costs of compliance; workforce retention and system stability.
specificPoints: Emphasize the system-level impact of burnout, where resources are diverted on a massive scale from patient care to administrative overhead, threatening the financial viability of "economically fragile hospitals".
Streamline duplicative documentation (e.g., care plans), reduce reporting burdens (e.g., HIPAA, PRO-PMs), and allow for more workforce flexibility (e.g., APP on-call).

Select Medical notes that hospitals spend "$3.1 million annually on average for CoP administrative requirements". GNYHA highlights the staffing and technology burdens of mandatory THA/TKA PRO-PM reporting.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Specific regulatory pain points like MIPS, prior authorization, and EHRs; loss of professional autonomy; time-suck of non-clinical tasks.
specificPoints: Provide hard data from member surveys on the time spent on administrative tasks and the direct link to burnout. The American Podiatric Medical Association reports clinicians spend over "200 hours per physician on MIPS-related activities". The Physicians Advocacy Institute cites that "nearly 90 percent of physicians report that prior authorization...increases physician burnout".
Repeal or significantly reform MIPS; streamline or eliminate prior authorization; standardize billing and credentialing; ban restrictive non-compete agreements for nurses.
The American Dental Association provides a comprehensive list of burdens unique to dentists, including inconsistent credentialing, Section 1557 taglines, and non-standardized HIPAA attachments.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Long-Term, Home, and Community Care Orgs
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Burdensome documentation tools (OASIS); punitive and inefficient survey/audit processes; workforce shortages for nurses and Direct Support Professionals (DSPs).
specificPoints: Focus on how "one-size-fits-all" regulations fail to account for the realities of their settings, eroding morale by "prioritizing paperwork over people". They highlight the disproportionate impact on smaller and rural providers.
Reform the survey process to be risk-based, reduce the documentation burden of tools like OASIS, and revise punitive penalty systems.

AHCA/NCAL argues the current oversight system "drives highly qualified and dedicated caregivers out of or away from the long term care profession". The National Alliance for Care at Home connects the OASIS burden directly to difficulty retaining skilled home health nurses.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Early Childhood & Mental Health Advocacy Groups
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Low compensation and high turnover; burdensome reporting requirements (e.g., GPRA); specific program rules (e.g., Head Start) that cause stress.
specificPoints: Broaden the burnout conversation to include educators and mental health providers, linking the crisis to societal undervaluation of their work and chronic underfunding.
Protect and increase compensation/wage parity provisions; streamline grant reporting requirements; provide more support for staff dealing with children with severe needs.

The Chance Project links burnout to the fact that "20% of Head Start and Early Head Start classrooms remain closed nationally due to workforce vacancies". The National Council for Mental Wellbeing notes that burdensome CMHC CoPs deter providers from offering new services like Intensive Outpatient Programs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The lived, daily experience of burnout; mental and physical exhaustion; feelings of failure and anxiety; physical risk.
specificPoints: Provide powerful, first-person narratives that humanize the abstract policy issue.
Focus on reforms that alleviate the direct sources of their personal stress.

An anonymous RN with ADHD describes the "mental torture" of struggling with an unmanaged condition and the "physical anxiety" of dealing with pharmacies monthly for medication. Dr. Ilana Slaff-Galatan highlights the severe physical injuries and risk faced by direct care workers who lack effective tools to manage aggressive behaviors.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Burnout as an Occupational Hazard: Dr. Ilana Slaff-Galatan makes a powerful and unique connection between regulatory bans on certain treatment tools (ESDs) and the direct physical endangerment of care workers. She argues the lack of effective interventions forces staff into dangerous situations, leading to severe injuries and trauma, reframing the issue from burnout to a matter of workplace safety.

The Compounding Effect on Small Practices: The American Dental Association and Dr. Courtney Younglove articulate how a multitude of 'minor' administrative burdens (credentialing, taglines, non-standard forms) creates a death-by-a-thousand-cuts scenario that is particularly punitive for small, independent practices that lack administrative staff, forcing them to divert clinical resources or stop accepting certain insurance.

The Personal Toll of System Failure: The anonymous comment from a registered nurse with ADHD provides a visceral account of how systemic barriers to care create a cycle of anxiety, self-blame, and exhaustion that directly impacts a professional's well-being and ability to care for others. It highlights that "people with ADHD are healthcare workers" themselves, suffering under the weight of a "broken" system.

Financial Data on Administrative Burden: The Pharmaceutical Printed Literature Association (PPLA) provides a striking data point: for a typical independent pharmacy, the cost of printing drug labeling as required by a proposed rule would account for "7.9% of owner discretionary profits," translating an abstract administrative burden into a concrete threat to financial viability.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type patterns: Views on the most pressing burdens differ significantly by care setting. Hospitals and large systems (AHA, GNYHA) focus on the cumulative weight of multiple large-scale reporting programs. Long-term care facilities (AHCA/NCAL, LeadingAge PA) focus intensely on the survey and penalty process. Small private practices (ADA, Younglove) are most concerned with the friction of daily operational tasks like credentialing and billing.

Experience correlations: Commenters who are themselves clinicians or represent specific clinical professions (e.g., Chustecki, APMA, ADA) tend to propose very granular, specific regulatory fixes. Commenters from large associations or think tanks (e.g., CPAC, AHCA/NCAL) are more likely to call for broader, systemic or philosophical changes to oversight.

Unintended consequences: Multiple commenters identify how regulations intended to improve quality or safety have backfired. The quality measure for antipsychotics in nursing homes has led to necessary medications being withheld (Project PAUSE). Punitive fines intended to improve quality risk closing facilities, harming residents and staff (AHCA/NCAL). Mandates for care of children with severe disabilities in Head Start without adequate resources lead to teacher burnout and retention issues (MiSide).

keyQuotations.quote: [The] increasing amount of administrative responsibility forced upon health care providers is unsustainable, diverts time and focus away from patient care and leads to provider burnout.
Every dollar spent on unnecessary bureaucracy is a dollar stolen from patient care. Every hour a provider spends complying with redundant rules is an hour not spent with a patient in need.
The current 'one-size-fits-all' schedule doesn’t reflect the reality, wasting valuable resources and eroding morale by prioritizing paperwork over people.
What it does drive is highly qualified and dedicated caregivers out of or away from the long term care profession, contributing to our sector’s ongoing workforce challenges.

Physicians spend 13 hours a week, on average, completing prior authorization requirements... and nearly 90 percent of physicians report that prior authorization somewhat or signiﬁcantly increases physician burnout.
I’m used to systems that are messy, but the system for ADHD care - especially for adults - isn’t just messy. It’s broken.

As a result, direct care workers, primarily women and people of color, daily risk their lives. They have suffered injuries requiring surgeries, finger amputations, and permanent opiate use for intractable pain.
Too many valued and highly qualified professionals feel discouraged by the lack of livable wages and choose to leave a field extremely important to determining the future well-being of our society.

Clinicians and administrators spend more than 200 hours per physician on MIPS-related activities, contributing to unsustainability of keeping up with ever-changing and overly burdensome regulatory requirements.
Excessive administrative tasks and constant compliance demands contribute to provider burnout, leading to higher turnover rates and difficulty retaining skilled staff.
The discordance between resources and responsibilities continues to exacerbate America’s primary care crisis.
For a typical independent pharmacy, the printing costs alone required under the FDA rule account for 0.4% of total revenue and 7.9% of owner discretionary profits.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment overwhelmingly views the current Post-Acute Care (PAC) regulatory framework as outdated, burdensome, and misaligned with modern clinical practice, creating significant barriers to efficient, patient-centered care. A powerful consensus exists for eliminating the three-day prior hospitalization rule for SNF admission. The primary tension lies between stakeholders seeking targeted deregulation of specific, costly rules (e.g., for IRFs, LTCHs) versus those advocating for broader systemic reforms, such as creating a unified PAC payment system and expanding the scope of practice for non-physician providers.

consensusPoints.text: The three-day prior hospitalization rule for SNF admission is obsolete and harmful. A strong majority of commenters, particularly hospitals, health systems, and provider associations, advocate for its complete elimination. They argue it is a clinically misaligned and outdated requirement that delays patient transitions, increases costs, and raises the risk of hospital-acquired complications. Mayo Clinic calls the rule a "clinical and operational barrier" and part of a "significantly outdated and clinically misaligned statutory and regulatory framework". AMGA states the requirement "reflects care patterns of an earlier era" and is now "both outdated and counterproductive". Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association notes that the temporary waiver during the COVID-19 PHE was effective and that hospitals, SNFs, and patient advocacy groups agree it should be eliminated.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Near-unanimous agreement among the 8 commenters who addressed it

exceptions.text: Support is for permanent, universal elimination. Some commenters note it has already been waived for certain CMMI models, citing this as evidence of its viability.

consensusPoints.text: Excessive administrative and reporting burdens divert resources from patient care. Most stakeholders across all PAC settings (LTCH, IRF, SNF, Home Health) and acute care hospitals report that duplicative, complex, and unnecessary documentation and data submission requirements are a primary concern. They argue these tasks consume staff time, increase operational costs, and contribute to clinician burnout without adding commensurate value to patient care or quality oversight. An LTC professional describes NHSN reporting as a "substantial administrative burden" where the "duplicative nature of data entry across systems takes away from direct resident care". AMRPA, representing IRFs, characterizes the IRF Quality Reporting Program as "byzantine" and the expansion of the IRF-PAI data set as "reporting for the sake of reporting". Select Medical, a major PAC provider, states they have been "forced to comply with many Federal regulations, guidance, paperwork requirements, and other regulatory obligations that are unnecessary, unlawful, unduly burdensome, and unsound".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (at least 12 of 24) explicitly cite burdensome regulations, paperwork, or reporting as a core problem.

areasOfDebate.topic: Expanding Scope of Practice for Non-Physician Providers in PAC Settings
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to grant Nurse Practitioners (NPs) and Physician Associates (PAs) the authority to perform duties currently reserved for physicians in SNFs and IRFs, such as initial admissions and comprehensive assessments.

positions.label: Expand Authority
positions.stance: Grant Nurse Practitioners (NPs) and Physician Associates (PAs) the authority to perform duties currently reserved for physicians in SNFs and IRFs, such as initial admissions and comprehensive assessments.
positions.supportLevel: All professional organizations representing NPs and PAs (AANP, AAPA).
keyArguments: Increases efficiency and patient access, especially in rural or underserved areas where physicians are scarce.
COVID-19 PHE waivers demonstrated that NPs and PAs can safely and effectively perform these duties, helping to address workforce shortages.
Studies show NP-led care in SNFs is associated with improved outcomes and lower costs, including reduced hospital readmissions.

positions.label: Maintain Status Quo
positions.stance: (Implicit Position) Retain current regulations requiring physician-led admission and care plan oversight in SNFs and IRFs.
positions.supportLevel: This is the current regulatory default; no commenters explicitly argued against expansion, but the existence of physician-specific requirements implies a historical rationale.
keyArguments: The complexity of PAC patients requires the specific diagnostic and management expertise of a physician for initial and comprehensive evaluations.
Maintaining physician oversight ensures a higher level of clinical accountability for the most vulnerable patients.
Changing this long-standing practice requires more evidence beyond temporary emergency waivers.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate is between focusing on repealing specific burdensome rules versus pursuing a fundamental redesign of the entire PAC system.

positions.label: Targeted Deregulation
positions.stance: Focus on repealing or modifying specific, highly burdensome, and low-value regulations within each PAC setting.
positions.supportLevel: Most facility-specific trade associations and some providers.
keyArguments: Provides immediate, tangible relief to providers by addressing their biggest pain points.
Avoids the complexity and delay of a massive systemic overhaul.
Focuses on rules with clear evidence of being outdated or ineffective, like the IRF Review Choice Demonstration or the LTCH 25-day LOS rule.

positions.label: Systemic Overhaul
positions.stance: Pursue a fundamental redesign of the PAC system, particularly through a unified, site-neutral payment and regulatory structure.
positions.supportLevel: Large integrated health systems and their associations.
keyArguments: The current fragmented structure creates perverse incentives and administrative complexity, hindering the ability to match patients to the right care setting based on need.
A unified system would simplify operations, improve data comparability, and reward efficiency across the care continuum.
Site-specific regulations are anachronistic; care should be dictated by patient needs, not facility type.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient throughput delays, administrative burdens in discharge planning, unreimbursed costs from extended stays caused by PAC bottlenecks.
specificPoints: View PAC regulations primarily through the lens of how they impact the transition of care from the hospital. They are most vocal about barriers like the SNF 3-day rule and prior authorization delays by payers, which trap patients in costly inpatient beds.
Proposed solutions: Eliminate the SNF 3-day rule, expand use of hospital swing beds, and simplify discharge requirements (e.g., the PAC provider list).
Cleveland Clinic and the American Hospital Association both cite the 3-day rule, prior authorization delays, and the cumbersome PAC provider list as key impediments to efficient patient discharge.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial viability threatened by payment policies (site-neutrality, rate cuts), excessive and setting-specific reporting burdens, and punitive audit programs.
specificPoints: Deeply focused on the operational and financial unsustainability of their specific care settings under current rules. They provide granular detail on how regulations like the IRF 60% Rule or LTCH payment policies directly lead to facility closures and reduced patient access.

Proposed solutions: Repeal site-neutral payments for LTCHs, terminate the IRF Review Choice Demonstration, prevent home health rate cuts, and streamline setting-specific documentation (e.g., IRF-PAI, SNF discharge notices).
NALTH directly links site-neutral policy to the closure of over 90 LTCHs. AMRPA provides a multi-page list of burdensome IRF-PAI data elements that should be removed.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Outdated scope of practice restrictions that prevent NPs and PAs from practicing to the full extent of their training and licensure.
specificPoints: Frame the issue as one of both workforce optimization and patient access. They are the only group to provide extensive data and studies on the positive outcomes associated with expanding NP/PA roles in PAC settings.
Proposed solutions: Permanently authorize NPs and PAs to perform initial admissions, comprehensive assessments, and other physician-delegated tasks in SNFs and IRFs.
The AANP cites numerous studies and a CMS Innovation Center report showing that increased NP presence in nursing homes reduces hospitalizations and costs while improving quality.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Removing barriers to specific care models (home-based care, telehealth) or addressing specific clinical issues (medication management).
specificPoints: Offer innovative or future-focused perspectives, arguing for modernizing regulations to enable new care delivery models. They connect regulatory burdens to a bias for institutional care.
Proposed solutions: Remove regulations that favor institutional settings, modernize face-to-face requirements to include telehealth, and align payment systems to support at-home care models.
Moving Health Home argues that "burdensome regulations that bias toward institutional care prevent innovative models like SNF at Home from growing."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Contradictory Policy Signals: Epic highlights a policy contradiction where CMS prohibits Home Health Agencies from using AI to improve efficiency in OASIS reporting, while simultaneously expanding the mandate for OASIS reporting, leaving HHAs "frustrated and left behind."

Compelling Personal Example: The comment from the individual LTC professional provides a powerful ground-level view, arguing that after years of mandatory reporting, facilities "have fully developed their internal infection prevention practices" and no longer need federal mandates that detract from care.

Data-backed Observation on Facility Closures: Select Medical and NALTH provide a stark, data-driven link between a specific policy—LTCH site-neutral payment—and a severe outcome: the closure of over 90 LTCHs and multiple provider bankruptcies.

Data-backed Observation on Facility Closures: Select Medical and NALTH provide a stark, data-driven link between a specific policy—LTCH site-neutral payment—and a severe outcome: the closure of over 90 LTCHs and multiple provider bankruptcies.

Unintended Consequence of Payer Behavior: The Federation of American Hospitals points out that Medicare Advantage plans' delays in prior authorization for PAC services are shifting costs back to acute care hospitals, who must absorb the cost of longer-than-necessary stays for patients "stuck in the hospital".

emergingPatterns.pattern: Views are heavily siloed by facility type. LTCHs focus on site-neutral payments and the 25-day LOS rule. IRFs focus on the 60% Rule, the RCD program, and the IRF-PAI burden. SNFs focus on the 3-day rule, discharge notices, and staffing regulations. Home Health agencies focus on payment rates and OASIS reporting.

Commenters who experienced regulatory flexibilities during the COVID-19 PHE (e.g., waiver of the 3-day rule, expanded NP/PA scope) universally advocate for making those changes permanent, citing the PHE as a successful real-world pilot program.

The National Rural Health Association (NRHA) and others highlight how regulations disproportionately affect rural areas. The need for swing bed flexibility and expanded NP/PA scope of practice is more acute where fewer PAC options and physicians are available.

Multiple commenters argue that policies intended to control costs in one area (e.g., site-neutral payments for LTCHs, prior authorization by MA plans) have created access crises and shifted costs and burdens to other parts of the system (acute care hospitals).

keyQuotations.quote: [The SNF 3-day stay requirement is a] significantly outdated and clinically misaligned statutory and regulatory framework that impedes best care for patients.
The perpetual requirement for routine NHSN reporting has become a substantial administrative burden for LTC staff already facing workforce shortages and high operational demands.
It is inexcusable that MAO are delaying or denying access to LTCHs for these patients. The costs are unacceptably high, with no benefit to the Medicare program.
CMS is simultaneously refusing to allow HHAs to improve their efficiency while creating newfound regulatory burden.

These settings are not large medical institutions - they are homes. Treating them as such requires regulatory flexibility and a modern understanding of how safety, accessibility, and autonomy coexist in community-based environments.
There are currently fewer than 330 LTCHs operating in the U.S., with more than 90 LTCHs closing since 2017 and the implementation of the site-neutral policy.
The expansion of the IRF-PAI with numerous unused data elements creates 'extensive and unnecessary administrative burden' that is 'reporting for the sake of reporting'.
If a Medicare beneficiary has been on a particular medication while a resident in the community, they should not lose access to that treatment just because they are admitted to a SNF.
The most frequently mentioned barrier for increasing the role of APRNs in nursing homes is their restricted scope of practice due to Medicare regulations.
Burdensome regulations that bias toward institutional care prevent innovative models like SNF at Home from growing.
The [enhanced barrier precautions] requirement is costly, administratively burdensome, and most importantly, negatively affects resident dignity and peace of mind.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on rural healthcare regulation is overwhelmingly critical, with commenters from all sectors asserting that one-size-fits-all federal policies are financially crippling rural providers and creating dangerous access-to-care deserts. The key tension exists between professional groups (nurses, PAs) seeking expanded scope of practice to fill workforce gaps and physician groups advocating for physician-led care models. The dominant thrust of recommendations is a call for immediate regulatory flexibility, targeted exemptions for rural providers, and the permanent adoption of telehealth to prevent the collapse of the rural health safety net.

consensusPoints.text: "One-Size-Fits-All" Regulations Are Detrimental to Rural Healthcare. Nearly all commenters argue that regulations designed for well-resourced urban health systems are inappropriate and actively harmful to rural providers. They contend these policies fail to account for unique rural challenges like workforce shortages, low patient volumes, limited infrastructure, and fragile financial stability. Representative Examples: OCHIN states, "Federal regulations designed for large, consolidated health systems often penalize providers in rural and low-resourced communities". The CPAC Foundation notes that "uniform compliance and quality reporting mandates are existential threats that deter participation in Medicare altogether" for rural providers. Select Medical argues that one-size-fits-all regulations "often disproportionately harm rural providers and limit access for beneficiaries in these communities".

exceptions.text: While the consensus is broad, the proposed solutions vary, from targeted exemptions to complete overhauls of specific rules.

consensusPoints.text: Telehealth is a Critical Lifeline for Rural Communities. A strong majority of stakeholders, including provider associations, advocacy groups, and individual practitioners, view telehealth as essential for overcoming geographic barriers, improving access to specialists, and ensuring continuity of care in rural areas. There is widespread support for making COVID-19-era telehealth flexibilities permanent. Representative Examples: The Alliance for Connected Care states, "Evidence shows telehealth helps reduce barriers to care, particularly for rural Americans, by connecting patients to services and specialists". An anonymous commenter noted they, living in a rural area, "never thought possible" to get diagnosed and treated for ADHD until telehealth became available. The Texas Association for Home Care and Hospice emphasizes that "Telehealth is especially critical for home health patient access in rural and underserved communities".

exceptions.text: Commenters also warn that access to telehealth is threatened by inadequate broadband infrastructure, restrictive state licensure laws, and high-friction identity-proofing requirements that disproportionately affect rural populations.

consensusPoints.text: The Financial Viability of Rural Providers is Tenuous and Worsening. A vast number of commenters, particularly hospital associations and advocacy groups, express alarm over the financial precarity of rural healthcare. They cite high rates of closures, negative operating margins, and the risk of creating "care deserts" as evidence that the current regulatory and reimbursement environment is unsustainable. Representative Examples: The National Rural Health Association (NRHA) provides stark data: "since 2010, almost 190 rural hospitals have closed... 46% of rural hospitals operate with negative margins, and 432 are identified as vulnerable to closure". The Physicians Advocacy Institute notes that between 2019 and 2024, "nearly 3,300 rural medical practices closed". R1 RCM cites an estimate that "30% of rural hospitals are in danger of closing due to financial difficulties".

areasOfDebate.topic: Anesthesia Care Models in Rural Hospitals
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to expand autonomy for Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetists (CRNAs) by removing physician supervision requirements to lower costs, or to ensure physician-led care by revising payment policies that currently incentivize non-physician anesthetists.

positions.label: Expand CRNA Autonomy
positions.stance: Remove physician supervision requirements for Certified Registered Nurse Anesthetists (CRNAs) to lower costs and increase staffing flexibility in rural facilities.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by the American Association of Nurse Anesthesiology (AANA).
keyArguments: Physician supervision requirements "drive up costs particularly for rural facilities"
in many states, CRNAs are the "sole anesthesia providers in nearly 100 percent of rural hospitals"
flexibility allows hospitals to select the most affordable and appropriate leader for their needs.

positions.label: Ensure Physician-Led Care
positions.stance: Revise payment policies to stop incentivizing non-physician anesthetists over anesthesiologists, ensuring rural patients have access to physician-led care.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by the American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA).
keyArguments: The current Rural Pass-Through program allows hospitals to receive cost-based Part A payments for CRNAs but not for physicians, which is "wrong" and "deprives rural citizens of physician care"
the program results in CRNAs being paid significantly more than under Part B, suggesting a need to scrutinize the program for cost savings.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on the implementation of site-neutral payment policies, with opposition from hospitals concerned about financial viability and reduced access, versus support for repurposing the savings to bolster primary care and rural initiatives.

positions.label: Oppose Site-Neutrality
positions.stance: Reject site-neutral payment policies because they fail to account for the higher regulatory burdens and 24/7 readiness costs of hospitals, which would force clinic closures and reduce rural access.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from hospital associations and post-acute care providers.
keyArguments: Policies would force closures of hospital outpatient clinics, "disproportionately affect[ing] rural areas"
they have already led to the loss of 22% of all Long-Term Care Hospitals (LTCHs), "exacerbating burdens in underserved areas"
they don't recognize that hospitals serve more medically complex and underserved populations.

positions.label: Repurpose Savings
positions.stance: Implement site-neutral payment reforms and redirect the federal savings toward expanding value-based primary care models and rural access initiatives.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by at least one academic physician.
keyArguments: This approach acknowledges the potential for savings while ensuring those funds are reinvested to solve the very access problems that opponents of the policy raise.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Burdensome Conditions of Participation (CoPs) like the CAH 96-hour rule and new OB requirements; financially damaging payment policies like site-neutrality; inflexible quality reporting (eCQMs, Star Ratings) that penalizes smaller facilities.
specificPoints: Emphasize the operational and financial unsustainability of current rules. They are the primary voice against the CAH 96-hour rule, citing its outdated nature and the success of its suspension during the PHE.
Proposed solutions: Eliminate the CAH 96-hour rule entirely; reject site-neutral payments; make eCQM reporting voluntary; repeal the 35-mile sole provider rule for CAH ambulance reimbursement.

NRHA cites data that 432 rural hospitals are vulnerable to closure. FAH argues that the 96-hour rule is "too restrictive and not clinically appropriate for many patients" in rural areas with limited transfer options.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Restrictive scope of practice laws; physician supervision requirements that act as costly, unnecessary barriers to care; lack of interstate licensure reciprocity.
specificPoints: They position their members (NPs, PAs, CRNAs) as the solution to rural workforce shortages, arguing that regulations, not a lack of providers, are the primary barrier to access.

Proposed solutions: Remove federal physician supervision requirements for APPs in RHCs, FQHCs, and CAHs; allow APPs to perform all duties within their state scope of practice (e.g., surgery in CAHs); expand interstate licensure compacts.

The AANP notes that during the PHE, waived supervision requirements "provid[ed] workforce flexibility in rural/underserved communities". The NCSBN cites data that APRNs in rural areas were 52% more likely to have to pay a fee for a mandatory collaborative agreement.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The creation of "care deserts" and "pharmacy deserts"; lack of access to specialty care (e.g., dialysis, mental health); the digital divide and its impact on access to care and information.
specificPoints: These groups connect regulatory burdens directly to the lived experience of rural citizens, highlighting issues like long travel times to the nearest pharmacy and the inability of local facilities to provide necessary services.

Proposed solutions: Ensure policy reflects "the real-world needs of rural Americans"; invest in broadband infrastructure; avoid mandates (like electronic-only prescribing info) that harm those without reliable internet.

PPLA warns that pharmacy closures mean rural patients may have to travel an "average 20 miles, up to 81 miles" to the next pharmacy. The National Grange states rural dialysis centers are "unequipped to handle" new bundling policies, leading to "reduced access, added red tape, and worse outcomes".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulations that create barriers to market entry or patient access for their products/services in rural areas (e.g., Coverage with Evidence Development, restrictive lab director requirements, high-friction identity proofing).
specificPoints: They highlight how specific technical or scientific regulations inadvertently create geographic disparities in access to innovation.

Proposed solutions: Rescind stricter lab director educational requirements; reform Coverage with Evidence Development (CED) to not concentrate trials in urban academic centers; lower identity-proofing thresholds for digital health access.

Eli Lilly points out that for one CED study, "18 states—many of them rural—had no New IDEAS site," forcing patients to travel across state lines. CareEvolution notes that high identity-proofing standards disproportionately affect rural residents who lack broadband or mobile devices for verification.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Multiple nursing organizations (AWHONN, NANN, ANA) point out a critical flaw in the Rural Emergency Hospital (REH) designation. HHS's interpretation that REHs cannot receive Medicaid reimbursement makes the designation a "death knell" for hospitals in high-Medicaid areas, completely undermining the program's intent to save rural facilities.

CareEvolution provides a unique insight, arguing that the high-friction IAL2 identity-proofing standard for accessing medical records is a form of digital redlining. It "disproportionately affects rural residents, who may lack broadband, a mobile device for selfies, or a private space for verification," effectively locking out the very populations who would most benefit from remote access.

The Food Is Health Revolution offers a historical perspective, tracing the origins of rural healthcare deficiencies back to the 1910 Flexner Report, which they argue "limited/eliminated rural healthcare" by centralizing medical education in university-affiliated hospitals and closing many smaller schools.

An anonymous individual managing ADHD in a rural area provides a powerful testimonial for telehealth, stating they "never thought possible" they could get diagnosed and treated until virtual care became an option, illustrating the transformative impact on individual lives.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-Type Specific Burdens: The comments reveal distinct clusters of regulatory pain points for different rural facility types. Critical Access Hospitals (CAHs) are universally burdened by the 96-hour rule. Rural Health Clinics (RHCs) are uniquely hampered by the "provider on-site" rule and inability to bill for group visits. Rural Emergency Hospitals (REHs) face an existential threat from the Medicaid reimbursement interpretation.

The Ripple Effect of Regulation: Commenters consistently trace a direct line from a specific regulation to a cascade of negative consequences. For example, Stark Law's site-of-service rules lead to specialists closing rural satellite offices (AMA), forcing patients to travel. Site-neutral payments lead to LTCH closures, reducing post-acute options in underserved areas (Select Medical).

Technology as a Double-Edged Sword: There is a clear pattern where technology is seen as both a primary solution and a source of new inequity. Telehealth is a lifeline, but access is threatened by the digital divide, licensure barriers, and identity protocols. Health IT reporting is intended to improve quality, but the cost and complexity disproportionately burden rural clinics without IT support.

The "Death by a Thousand Cuts" Phenomenon: No single regulation is solely responsible for the crisis.
